compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature pay is PERMITTED but CAPPED. AS 15.45.110(c): a circulator may not receive (and a person or organization may not pay) more than $1 per signature for the collection of signatures on a petition. AS 15.45.110(d) separately bars paying anyone to sign or refrain from signing. Violation of (c) or (d) is a class B misdemeanor (AS 15.45.110(e)). The circulator affidavit (

voterFile.cost: ~$20 for the statewide voter list (price estimate per the U.S. EAC compilation; the Alaska Division of Elections does not publish a per-record fee schedule online and quotes/processes orders through a divisional office). Treat $20 as an estimate, not a guaranteed published figure.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — Alaska voter registration records are an "Open" file. Names, addresses, and party affiliation are public and available to anyone who purchases the list (candidates, parties, committees, and the public alike). A voter may individually elect to keep their residential address confidential by providing a separate mailing address (AS 15.07.195(b)).
voterFile.useRestrictions: No statutory eligibility gate, but per the EAC compilation, "even the least restrictive states prohibit the commercial use of voter file information." The file excludes confidential PII (DOB/age, SSN, DL number, voter ID number, place of birth, signature) under AS 15.07.195(a). Specific penalty/affidavit language for commercial-use prohibition was not located on the primary Division of Elections page during this research.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written/in-person request to a Division of Elections divisional office — NOT an instant online download. The Division's Research page states lists are "available for purchase" and to "contact any divisional office for assistance." No public self-serve download portal for the bulk statewide file was found.

Governor's official figures are all primary-source-grounded and mutually consistent, so the RULES are settled. HOLD is assigned only on the precise current-cycle NUMBER: the authoritative figure currently in force is 34,098 valid signatures across at least 30 of 40 house districts (10% of 2022 general election turnout; 7% per district), confirmed by the Lt. Governor's Dec. 31,

citations.quote: After certification of the application, a petition containing a summary of the subject matter shall be prepared by the lieutenant governor for circulation by the sponsors. If signed by qualified voters who are equal in number to at least ten percent of those who voted in the preceding general election, who are resident in at least three-fourths of the house districts of the State, and who, in each of those house districts, are equal in number to at least seven percent of those who voted in the preceding general election in the house district, it may be filed with the lieutenant governor. [Amend 1970, 1998, and 2004]

(a) The sponsors must file the initiative petition within one year from the time the sponsors received notice from the lieutenant governor that the petitions were ready for delivery to them. The petition may be filed with the lieutenant governor only if it meets all of the following requirements: it is signed by qualified voters (1) equal in number to 10 percent of those who voted in the preceding general election; (2) resident in at least three-fourths of the house districts of the state; and (3) who, in each of the house districts described in (2) of this subsection, are equal in number to at least seven percent of those who voted in the preceding general election in the house district. (b) If the petition is not filed within the one-year period provided for in (a) of this section, the petition has no force or effect.

After a thorough review, the Division of Elections confirmed that 42,837 qualified signatures were verified across 40 house districts, exceeding the required minimum of 34,098 qualified signatures across 30 house districts.

the initiative will appear as a proposition on the ballot at the first statewide election held after 120 days have passed since the adjournment of the legislative session (AS 15.45.190). Practically, this means that unless the 2026 legislative session adjourns on or before April 20, 2026, or substantially similar legislation is enacted before the election, the proposition will appear on the November 3, 2026, general election ballot.

An initiative petition may be filed at any time. The lieutenant governor shall prepare a ballot title and proposition summarizing the proposed law, and shall place them on the ballot for the first statewide election held more than one hundred twenty days after adjournment of the legislative session following the filing. If, before the election, substantially the same measure has been enacted, the petition is void.

A referendum petition may be filed only within ninety days after adjournment of the legislative session at which the act was passed. The lieutenant governor shall prepare a ballot title and proposition summarizing the act and shall place them on the ballot for the first statewide election held more than one hundred eighty days after adjournment of that session.

The initiative shall not be used to dedicate revenues, make or repeal appropriations, create courts, define the jurisdiction of courts or prescribe their rules, or enact local or special legislation. The referendum shall not be applied to dedications of revenue, to appropriations, to local or special legislation, or to laws necessary for the immediate preservation of the public peace, health, or safety.
To circulate a petition booklet, a person shall be (1) a citizen of the United States; (2) 18 years of age or older; and (3) a resident of the state as determined under AS 15.05.020.

(a) The petitions may be circulated throughout the state only in person. ... (c) A circulator may not receive payment or agree to receive payment that is greater than $1 a signature, and a person or an organization may not pay or agree to pay an amount that is greater than $1 a signature, for the collection of signatures on a petition. (d) A person or organization may not knowingly pay, offer to pay, or cause to be paid money or other valuable thing to a person to sign or refrain from signing a petition. (e) A person or organization that violates (c) or (d) of this section is guilty of a class B misdemeanor.

Before being filed, each petition shall be certified by an affidavit by the person who personally circulated the petition. ... The affidavit must state in substance (1) that the person signing the affidavit meets the residency, age, and citizenship qualifications for circulating a petition under AS 15.45.105; (2) that the person is the only circulator of that petition; (3) that the signatures were made in the circulator's actual presence; ... (8) whether the circulator has received payment or agreed to receive payment for the collection of signatures on the petition, and, if so, the name of each person or organization that has paid or agreed to pay the circulator for collection of signatures on the petition.

signatures.headline: "ten percent (10%) of the total number of votes cast for the office of governor in the last preceding general election" (Ark. Const. Art. 5 § 1; SoS Dec-2025 handbook). Base = 2022 gubernatorial general election.

deadline.headline: Publication: "Wednesday, June 3, 2026 – Deadline for publication of a statewide initiative measure in a newspaper of general (statewide) circulation... at least thirty (30) days before filing." Filing: "Friday, July 3, 2026 – Deadline to file statewide initiative measures with the Secretary of State... not less than four (4) months before the November 2026 General Election."

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Per-signature pay for OBTAINING signatures is statutorily prohibited (consistent with Ballotpedia's list of ~10 states that ban per-sig pay, which includes AR). Ark. Code Ann. § 7-9-601(g), as reproduced verbatim in the SoS Dec-2025 handbook: "(1) It is unlawful for a person to pay or offer to pay a person, or receive payment or agree to receive payment, on a basis related to t

voterFile.cost: Up to $50 per county on electronic media (≤5,000 voters $10; 5,001–25,000 $25; >25,000 $50); printed lists ≤2¢/name. No statewide SoS file — assembled county-by-county across 75 county clerks (Ark. Code 7-5-109).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public. Ark. Code 7-5-109 says the county clerk "shall provide the list" upon request and imposes no eligibility limit (not restricted to candidates, committees, parties, or political use). The list is a public record subject only to redaction of "secure voter" addresses.
voterFile.useRestrictions: The statute imposes no commercial-use ban or political-use-only limit on the registered voter list itself. The one carve-out is "secure voters": county clerks "shall not reproduce any address of a secure voter in any format unless otherwise authorized by law," and all furnished lists exclude secure-voter addresses (Ark. Code 7-5-109(a)(2), (c)). No penalty for ordinary use is specified in this section.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written / in-person request to the county clerk in each county (the statewide list is assembled county-by-county; the statewide voter registration database is maintained centrally but the statutory access mechanism is the county clerk). No notarized affidavit or sworn statement of permitted use is required by statute. Delivered on compact disc or other electronic medium, or as a printed list.

verification.riskNote: Arkansas has a robust direct citizen-initiative process under Art. 5 § 1 of the 1874 Constitution (originally Amendment 7): direct initiated constitutional amendments (10%), direct initiated statutes/"initiated acts" (8%), and veto/popular referenda against legislative acts (6%) — all computed on the total votes cast for Governor in the last preceding gubernatorial general election. Because AR elects its governor in midterm years, the current-cycle base for the November 2026

citations.quote: Eight per cent of the legal voters may propose any law and ten per cent may propose a constitutional amendment by initiative petition... any number not less than six per cent of the legal voters may, by petition, order the referendum against any general Act... The total number of votes cast for the office of Governor in the last preceding general election shall be the basis upon which the number of signatures of legal voters upon state-wide initiative and referendum petitions shall be computed.

Upon all initiative or referendum petitions provided for in any of the sections of this article, it shall be necessary to file from at least fifteen of the counties of the State, petitions bearing the signature of not less than one-half of the designated percentage of the electors of such county.

Constitutional Amendment – 90,704 signatures, with sufficient signatures from each of at least fifty (50) counties of the state... Initiated Act – 72,563 signatures, with sufficient signatures from each of at least fifty (50) counties of the state... Statewide Referendum – 54,422 signatures, with sufficient signatures from each of at least fifty (50) counties of the state

In addition to the minimum number of signatures, the petition, at the time of filing, must contain the signatures of qualified electors from at least fifty (50) counties in the state. This requirement has historically been interpreted as requiring that each of these fifty (50) counties' petitions must contain the signatures of not less than one half of the designated percentage of the electors from that county (1/2 of 10% = 5% for a constitutional amendment; and 1/2 of 8% = 4% for an act; 1/2 of 6% = 3% for a referendum).

citations.field: deadline (initiative publication and filing for Nov 2026)
citations.quote: Wednesday, June 3, 2026 – Deadline for publication of a statewide initiative measure in a newspaper of general (statewide) circulation, at the expense of petitioners, to place the measure on the ballot for the November 2026 General Election... Friday, July 3, 2026 – Deadline to file statewide initiative measures with the Secretary of State to place the measure on the ballot for the November 2026 General Election.

Such petition shall be filed with the Secretary of State not later than ninety days after the final adjournment of the session at which such Act was passed, except when a recess or adjournment shall be taken temporarily for a longer period than ninety days, in which case such petition shall be filed not later than ninety days after such recess or temporary adjournment.

The Canvasser does not have to be a registered voter, but the canvasser must be an Arkansas resident, at least eighteen (18) years of age, and a U.S. citizen.

(g) (1) It is unlawful for a person to pay or offer to pay a person, or receive payment or agree to receive payment, on a basis related to the number of signatures obtained on a statewide initiative petition, or statewide referendum petition, local initiative petition, or local referendum petition. (2) This subsection does not prohibit compensation for circulating petitions but only compensation for obtaining signatures when the compensation or compensation level is impacted by or related to the number of signatures obtained. (3) A signature obtained in violation of this subsection is void and shall not be counted. (4) A violation under this subsection is a Class A misdemeanor.

citations.field: perSignaturePayAllowed (constitutional anti-restriction clause permitting paid circulation generally)
citations.quote: Unwarranted Restrictions Prohibited. No law shall be passed to prohibit any person or persons from giving or receiving compensation for circulating petitions, nor to prohibit the circulation of petitions, nor in any manner interfering with the freedom of the people in procuring petitions; but laws shall be enacted prohibiting and penalizing perjury, forgery, and all other felonies or other fraudulent practices, in the securing of signatures or filing of petitions.
NOTE 2 – At the time this publication was revised, litigation was ongoing concerning Acts 218, 240, 241, 274, and 453 of 2025, Act 236 of 2023, and Ark. Code Ann. § 7-9-103, 113, 126, and 601.

citations.field: distribution (facial invalidity if 50-county minimum not met at filing)
citations.quote: At the time of filing, the petition must contain the requisite number of signatures, or the petition will be determined to be facially invalid and rejected without the verification of any signatures. In other words, an initiated amendment petition must contain at least 90,704 signatures (also referred to as the initial count) and the required minimum numbers from each of at least fifty (50) counties, or it will be rejected without the verification of any signatures.

(g) (1) It is unlawful for a person to pay or offer to pay a person, or receive payment or agree to receive payment, on a basis related to the number of signatures obtained on a statewide initiative petition, or statewide referendum petition, local initiative petition, or local referendum petition. (2) This subsection does not prohibit compensation for circulating petitions but only compensation for obtaining signatures when the compensation or compensation level is impacted by or related to the number of signatures obtained. (3) A signature obtained in violation of this subsection is void and shall not be counted. (4) A violation under this subsection is a Class A misdemeanor.

citations.field: perSignaturePayAllowed (constitutional anti-restriction clause permitting paid circulation generally)
citations.quote: Unwarranted Restrictions Prohibited. No law shall be passed to prohibit any person or persons from giving or receiving compensation for circulating petitions, nor to prohibit the circulation of petitions, nor in any manner interfering with the freedom of the people in procuring petitions; but laws shall be enacted prohibiting and penalizing perjury, forgery, and all other felonies or other fraudulent practices, in the securing of signatures or filing of petitions.

Statutory 50-county requirement: "sufficient signatures from each of at least fifty (50) counties of the state," interpreted as not less than one-half the designated percentage per county (1/2 of 6% = 3%). Failure at filing = facially invalid, rejected without verification.

Per-signature pay for OBTAINING signatures is statutorily prohibited (consistent with Ballotpedia's list of ~10 states that ban per-sig pay, which includes AR). Ark. Code Ann. § 7-9-601(g), as reproduced verbatim in the SoS Dec-2025 handbook: "(1) It is unlawful for a person to pay or offer to pay a person, or receive payment or agree to receive payment, on a basis related to t

voterFile.cost: Up to $50 per county on electronic media (≤5,000 voters $10; 5,001–25,000 $25; >25,000 $50); printed lists ≤2¢/name. No statewide SoS file — assembled county-by-county across 75 county clerks (Ark. Code 7-5-109).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public. Ark. Code 7-5-109 says the county clerk "shall provide the list" upon request and imposes no eligibility limit (not restricted to candidates, committees, parties, or political use). The list is a public record subject only to redaction of "secure voter" addresses.
voterFile.useRestrictions: The statute imposes no commercial-use ban or political-use-only limit on the registered voter list itself. The one carve-out is "secure voters": county clerks "shall not reproduce any address of a secure voter in any format unless otherwise authorized by law," and all furnished lists exclude secure-voter addresses (Ark. Code 7-5-109(a)(2), (c)). No penalty for ordinary use is specified in this section.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written / in-person request to the county clerk in each county (the statewide list is assembled county-by-county; the statewide voter registration database is maintained centrally but the statutory access mechanism is the county clerk). No notarized affidavit or sworn statement of permitted use is required by statute. Delivered on compact disc or other electronic medium, or as a printed list.

verification.riskNote: Arkansas has a robust direct citizen-initiative process under Art. 5 § 1 of the 1874 Constitution (originally Amendment 7): direct initiated constitutional amendments (10%), direct initiated statutes/"initiated acts" (8%), and veto/popular referenda against legislative acts (6%) — all computed on the total votes cast for Governor in the last preceding gubernatorial general election. Because AR elects its governor in midterm years, the current-cycle base for the November 2026

citations.quote: Eight per cent of the legal voters may propose any law and ten per cent may propose a constitutional amendment by initiative petition... any number not less than six per cent of the legal voters may, by petition, order the referendum against any general Act... The total number of votes cast for the office of Governor in the last preceding general election shall be the basis upon which the number of signatures of legal voters upon state-wide initiative and referendum petitions shall be computed.

Upon all initiative or referendum petitions provided for in any of the sections of this article, it shall be necessary to file from at least fifteen of the counties of the State, petitions bearing the signature of not less than one-half of the designated percentage of the electors of such county.

Constitutional Amendment – 90,704 signatures, with sufficient signatures from each of at least fifty (50) counties of the state... Initiated Act – 72,563 signatures, with sufficient signatures from each of at least fifty (50) counties of the state... Statewide Referendum – 54,422 signatures, with sufficient signatures from each of at least fifty (50) counties of the state

In addition to the minimum number of signatures, the petition, at the time of filing, must contain the signatures of qualified electors from at least fifty (50) counties in the state. This requirement has historically been interpreted as requiring that each of these fifty (50) counties' petitions must contain the signatures of not less than one half of the designated percentage of the electors from that county (1/2 of 10% = 5% for a constitutional amendment; and 1/2 of 8% = 4% for an act; 1/2 of 6% = 3% for a referendum).

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Per-signature pay is explicitly BANNED for statewide initiatives and referenda. ARS 19-118.01(A): "A person shall not pay or receive money or any other thing of value based on the number of signatures collected on a statewide initiative or referendum petition. Signatures that are obtained by a paid circulator who violates this section are void and shall not be counted in determ

voterFile.cost: Statutory tiered formula (ARS 16-168(E)): 1–124,999 records = $93.75 + $0.0005/record; 125,000–249,999 = $156.25 + $0.000375/record; 250,000–499,999 = $203.13 + $0.00025/record; 500,000–999,999 = $265.63 + $0.000125/record; 1,000,000+ records = $328.13 + $0.0000625/record. A full statewide file (~4.3M registered voters) falls in the top tier: ~$328.13 + $0.0000625/record (on the order of a few hundred dollars total).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Not the general public for any purpose. Free electronic copies go to county/state party chairmen of qualifying parties. Anyone else (candidates, registered political committees, etc.) may purchase copies only for a statutorily "authorized use" — i.e. political/political-party activity, a political campaign, an election, redistricting, signature verification on petitions/candidate filings, or another use specifically authorized by law. Commercial use is prohibited.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-use-only: data "may be used only for purposes relating to a political or political party activity, a political campaign or an election... and may not be used for a commercial purpose." Signature verification on petitions and candidate filings is expressly authorized. Reselling/transferring for an unauthorized purpose or posting it on the internet is prohibited. A violation of the use restriction or resale/distribution restriction "is guilty of a class 6 felony." Sensitive fields (full DOB, SSN, driver license number, voter signature, email) are excluded from general reproduction but may be used for petition signature verification.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request for an authorized use submitted to the county recorder (or the Secretary of State / officer in charge of elections); the office prepares additional copies and furnishes them within 30 days of receipt of the request upon payment of the statutory fee. (Verifying petition signatures is an explicitly permitted use, so a campaign/committee qualifies.)

verification.riskNote: Primary sources are the Arizona Constitution Article 4, Part 1, Section 1 (self-executing initiative/referendum provision) and ARS Title 19, both from the official azleg.gov legislature site, and they fully and unambiguously establish the formulas (15%/10%/5%), the base (votes cast for all gubernatorial candidates at the last preceding general election), the deadlines (initiative: not less than four months before the election; referendum: not more than 90 days after legislati

citations.quote: The first of these reserved powers is the initiative.  Under this power ten percent of the qualified electors shall have the right to propose any measure, and fifteen percent shall have the right to propose any amendment to the constitution.

The second of these reserved powers is the referendum. Under this power the legislature, or five percent of the qualified electors, may order the submission to the people at the polls of any measure, or item, section or part of any measure, enacted by the legislature

The whole number of votes cast for all candidates for governor at the general election last preceding the filing of any initiative or referendum petition on a state or county measure shall be the basis on which the number of qualified electors required to sign such petition shall be computed.

All petitions submitted under the power of the initiative shall be known as initiative petitions, and shall be filed with the secretary of state not less than four months preceding the date of the election at which the measures so proposed are to be voted upon.  All petitions submitted under the power of the referendum ... shall be filed with the secretary of state not more than ninety days after the final adjournment of the session of the legislature which shall have passed the measure to which the referendum is applied.

Every initiative measure shall embrace but one subject and matters properly connected therewith, which subject shall be expressed in the title; but if any subject shall be embraced in an initiative measure which shall not be expressed in the title, such initiative measure shall be void only as to so much thereof as shall not be embraced in the title.

A person shall not pay or receive money or any other thing of value based on the number of signatures collected on a statewide initiative or referendum petition.  Signatures that are obtained by a paid circulator who violates this section are void and shall not be counted in determining the legal sufficiency of the petition.

For statewide initiative and referendum measures only, all circulators who are not residents of this state and all paid circulators must register as circulators with the secretary of state before circulating petitions pursuant to this title.

voterFile.cost: $100 flat application fee for the statewide voter registration information file (one price for the whole state, regardless of record count). Single-voter records (≤10) are $30.00/record up to a $100 max. County-specific files vary by county.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated, NOT general public. Per the SoS: "any candidate for federal, state, or local office, any committee for or against any initiative or referendum measure for which legal publication is made, any person for election, scholarly, journalistic, or political purposes, and any person for governmental purposes, as determined by the Secretary of State." A petition-signature-verification campaign/committee qualifies under the election/political-purpose and candidate/committee categories.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strong. Use is limited to permitted (election/political/scholarly/journalistic/governmental) purposes only; commercial use is prohibited. Misuse is a crime: Elections Code 18109(a) makes it "a misdemeanor for a person in possession of information ... obtained pursuant to Article 5 ... knowingly to use or permit the use of all or any part of that information for any purpose other than as permitted by law," and 18109(b) makes obtaining/using the data without first complying with the application requirements of 2188 a misdemeanor. The application must state intended use and is certified "under penalty of perjury." Confidential-voter (Safe at Home, etc.) records are excluded entirely.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form + approval (not an instant download). Submit the "California Voter Registration File Request" application (PDF) to the SoS. Elections Code 2188 requires a signed application stating the requestor's name, residence/business address, ID number, the specific information requested, and a statement of intended use, certified "under penalty of perjury." SoS reviews to confirm the use is legally permitted: "requests can take 7-14 business days to review and process." No refunds once sent.

citations.quote: The current initiative signature requirements according to the California Constitution, Article II, Section 8(b) and Elections Code section 9035 are as follows: Initiative Statute: 546,651 Initiative Constitutional Amendment: 874,641

An initiative measure may be proposed by presenting to the Secretary of State a petition that sets forth the text of the proposed statute or amendment to the Constitution and is certified to have been signed by electors equal in number to 5 percent in the case of a statute, and 8 percent in the case of an amendment to the Constitution, of the votes for all candidates for Governor at the last gubernatorial election.

is certified to have been signed by registered voters equal in number to 5 percent in the case of a statute, and 8 percent in the case of an amendment to the Constitution, of the voters for all candidates for Governor at the last gubernatorial election preceding the issuance of the circulating title and summary for the initiative measure by the Attorney General.
An initiative measure embracing more than one subject may not be submitted to the electors or have any effect.
The Secretary of State shall then submit the measure at the next general election held at least 131 days after it qualifies or at any special statewide election held prior to that general election.

a petition with signatures for a proposed initiative measure shall be filed with the county elections official not later than 180 days from the official summary date, and a county elections official shall not accept a petition for the proposed initiative measure after that period.

The petitions must be signed by registered voters in an amount equal to at least 5% of the votes cast for all candidates for Governor at the last gubernatorial election, which was held on November 8, 2022. The total number of valid signatures required to qualify a referendum for the ballot is 546,651. (Cal. Const., art. II, § 9(b).)

A referendum measure may be proposed by presenting to the Secretary of State, within 90 days after the enactment date of the statute, a petition certified to have been signed by electors equal in number to 5 percent of the votes for all candidates for Governor at the last gubernatorial election, asking that the statute or part of it be submitted to the electors.
A person shall not circulate a state or local initiative, referendum, or recall petition or nominating paper unless the person is 18 years of age or older.

Every person who offers or gives money or other valuable consideration to another in exchange for his or her signature on a state, county, municipal, or district initiative, referendum, or recall petition is guilty of a misdemeanor.

signatures.headline: Article V, Section 1(2) / 1(2.5): "at least five percent of the total number of votes cast for all candidates for the office of secretary of state at the previous general election"; AND for constitutional amendments only, an additional distribution requirement of "at least 2% of the total registered electors in each of the 35 Colorado state senate districts" (Colo. Const. Art. V, Sec. 1(2.5)).

deadline.headline: Per CRS 1-40-108(1): filed with the Secretary of State within six months from the date titles/submission clause were fixed AND "no later than three months before the election at which it is to be voted upon." For the November 3, 2026 general election, the SoS deadline is August 3, 2026 (corroborated by Ballotpedia 2026 deadlines table: 08/03/2026).

Const. Art. V, Sec. 1(2.5)).

For the November 3, 2026 general election, the SoS deadline is August 3, 2026 (corroborated by Ballotpedia 2026 deadlines table: 08/03/2026).

For constitutional amendments only: at least 2% of the total registered electors in each of the 35 Colorado state senate districts (Colo. Const. Art. V, Sec. 1(2.5); the SoS guide also cites Art. V, Sec. 1(4)). The per-district signature count is provided to the designated representatives when the petition format is approved.

compensation.operatingStance: Colorado expressly permits paid circulators. CRS 1-40-112(2)(b) requires a paid circulator to wear a \"PAID CIRCULATOR\" badge with the employer's name and phone number; volunteers wear a \"VOLUNTEER CIRCULATOR\" badge. Entities that pay circulators are \"petition entities\" that must obtain a license from the Secretary of State before compensating circulators (CRS 1-40-135). C

voterFile.cost: $50.00 flat for the Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003), delivered on data disc (per report). Alternatively, a full Election Cycle Subscription Service (weekly-updated EX-003 plus other extracts for the entire statewide election cycle) is $1,000.00. Free public online lookups exist only for individual voters, not the bulk file.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — no eligibility gate. Colorado voter registration records are statutorily "public records subject to examination by any person." The Elections Data Request Form asks only for requestor name/company and contact info; it is not limited to candidates, committees, or parties.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Social Security numbers, driver's license/state ID numbers, the SOS-assigned unique voter ID, and electronic copies of voter signatures are statutorily excluded from any release (C.R.S. 1-2-302(8)); preregistrant (under-18) information is kept confidential. Colorado law (C.R.S. 1-2-227 and related statutes) restricts use of voter registration data to election/political purposes and prohibits using it for commercial solicitation; the released file does not contain SSN or signature data, so it supports name/address matching but not signature-image comparison.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form by mail. Complete the Colorado Department of State "Elections Data Request Form" (PDF), check the box for "Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003)," enclose a check or money order payable to the Colorado Secretary of State, and mail it to the SOS Elections Division (1700 Broadway, Suite 550, Denver, CO 80290). Requests are returned via first-class mail within 72 hours; for expedited carriers the requestor must include a pre-paid mailer. Subscription service provides electronic download access.

verification.riskNote: All core figures and rules are grounded in primary/authoritative Colorado sources: the Secretary of State's official signature-requirements and filing-guide pages (which quote Colo. Const. Art. V, Sec. 1 verbatim) and the statute (CRS Title 1, Article 40) via FindLaw. Current-cycle signature requirement = 124,238 valid signatures (5% x 2,484,758 votes cast for SoS in the Nov 8, 2022 general election), applicable 2023-2026 for both initiatives and statewide referenda. citations.quote: According to Article V, Section 1(2) of the Colorado Constitution, the number of signatures required for a statewide initiative petition is "at least five percent of the total number of votes cast for all candidates for the office of secretary of state at the previous general election". Article V, Section 1(3) of the Colorado Constitution also requires the same number of signatures for a statewide referendum petition. The total number of votes cast for all candidates for the office of secretary of state at the November 8, 2022 General Election was 2,484,758. As a result, the signature requirement for statewide initiative petitions and statewide referendum petitions for 2023 through 2026 is: 124,238

Initiatives that wish to make a change to the Colorado constitution must be signed by at least 2% of the total registered electors in each of the 35 Colorado state senate districts in addition to meeting the requirements outlined above (Article V, Section 1(2.5) of the Colorado constitution).

In the November 8, 2022 General Election, there were 2,484,758 votes cast for the office: (2,484,758) x (.05) = (124,238). Therefore, the signature requirement for state initiatives for 2023-2026 is: 124,238

No petition for any ballot issue is of any effect unless filed with the secretary of state within six months from the date that the titles and submission clause have been fixed and determined pursuant to the provisions of sections 1-40-106 and 1-40-107 and unless filed with the secretary of state no later than three months before the election at which it is to be voted upon.

Starting on the date the Title Board set the final language, proponents have six months to circulate petitions. However, the Colorado Constitution requires petitions to be filed no later than three months (August) before an election.

Every proposed constitutional amendment or statutory proposition must be limited to a single subject, which must be clearly expressed in its title. In other words, the text of the measure must concern only one subject and one distinct purpose. For additional information relating to the single-subject requirement, see Article V, Section 1(5.5) of the Colorado Constitution and section 1-40-106.5, C.R.S.

(1) No person shall circulate a petition for an initiative or referendum measure unless the person is a citizen of the United States and at least eighteen years of age at the time the petition is circulated. (2)(b) A circulator who is to be paid for circulating a petition concerning a ballot issue shall display an identification badge that includes the words "PAID CIRCULATOR" in bold-faced type that is clearly legible and the name and telephone number of the individual employing the circulator. ... (4) Repealed by Laws 2018, Ch. 113, sec. 3, eff. Aug. 8, 2018.

The Colorado Revised Statutes define a petition entity as any person or issue committee that provides compensation to a circulator to circulate a ballot petition. ... It is unlawful for any petition entity to provide compensation to a circulator to circulate a petition without first obtaining a license from the Secretary of State.

No petition for any ballot issue is of any effect unless filed with the secretary of state within six months from the date that the titles and submission clause have been fixed and determined pursuant to the provisions of sections 1-40-106 and 1-40-107 and unless filed with the secretary of state no later than three months before the election at which it is to be voted upon.

Starting on the date the Title Board set the final language, proponents have six months to circulate petitions. However, the Colorado Constitution requires petitions to be filed no later than three months (August) before an election.

Every proposed constitutional amendment or statutory proposition must be limited to a single subject, which must be clearly expressed in its title. In other words, the text of the measure must concern only one subject and one distinct purpose. For additional information relating to the single-subject requirement, see Article V, Section 1(5.5) of the Colorado Constitution and section 1-40-106.5, C.R.S.

(1) No person shall circulate a petition for an initiative or referendum measure unless the person is a citizen of the United States and at least eighteen years of age at the time the petition is circulated. (2)(b) A circulator who is to be paid for circulating a petition concerning a ballot issue shall display an identification badge that includes the words "PAID CIRCULATOR" in bold-faced type that is clearly legible and the name and telephone number of the individual employing the circulator. ... (4) Repealed by Laws 2018, Ch. 113, sec. 3, eff. Aug. 8, 2018.

The Colorado Revised Statutes define a petition entity as any person or issue committee that provides compensation to a circulator to circulate a ballot petition. ... It is unlawful for any petition entity to provide compensation to a circulator to circulate a petition without first obtaining a license from the Secretary of State.

compensation.operatingStance: Colorado expressly permits paid circulators. CRS 1-40-112(2)(b) requires a paid circulator to wear a \"PAID CIRCULATOR\" badge with the employer's name and phone number; volunteers wear a \"VOLUNTEER CIRCULATOR\" badge. Entities that pay circulators are \"petition entities\" that must obtain a license from the Secretary of State before compensating circulators (CRS 1-40-135). C

voterFile.cost: $50.00 flat for the Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003), delivered on data disc (per report). Alternatively, a full Election Cycle Subscription Service (weekly-updated EX-003 plus other extracts for the entire statewide election cycle) is $1,000.00. Free public online lookups exist only for individual voters, not the bulk file.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — no eligibility gate. Colorado voter registration records are statutorily "public records subject to examination by any person." The Elections Data Request Form asks only for requestor name/company and contact info; it is not limited to candidates, committees, or parties.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Social Security numbers, driver's license/state ID numbers, the SOS-assigned unique voter ID, and electronic copies of voter signatures are statutorily excluded from any release (C.R.S. 1-2-302(8)); preregistrant (under-18) information is kept confidential. Colorado law (C.R.S. 1-2-227 and related statutes) restricts use of voter registration data to election/political purposes and prohibits using it for commercial solicitation; the released file does not contain SSN or signature data, so it supports name/address matching but not signature-image comparison.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form by mail. Complete the Colorado Department of State "Elections Data Request Form" (PDF), check the box for "Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003)," enclose a check or money order payable to the Colorado Secretary of State, and mail it to the SOS Elections Division (1700 Broadway, Suite 550, Denver, CO 80290). Requests are returned via first-class mail within 72 hours; for expedited carriers the requestor must include a pre-paid mailer. Subscription service provides electronic download access.

Current-cycle signature requirement = 124,238 valid signatures (5% x 2,484,758 votes cast for SoS in the Nov 8, 2022 general election), applicable 2023-2026 for both initiatives and statewide referenda. 5% of registered qualified electors in each of 5 or more of the 8 wards (in addition to 5% citywide). Per-ward 5% as of Jan 31, 2026 (ceil): W1 2,896; W2 2,621; W3 2,847; W4 3,051; W5 3,442; W6 3,419; W7 3,052; W8 2,487.

No express single-subject rule. Subject limits instead: an initiative may NOT appropriate funds (§ 1-1001.02(10)), may NOT authorize discrimination prohibited under Chapter 14 of Title 2 (§ 1-1001.16(b)(1)(C)), and may NOT negate or limit the Council's budget act under § 1-204.46 (§ 1-1001.16(b)(1)(D)).

compensation.operatingStance: DC does NOT ban paying circulators per-signature. The DC election code's compensation crime, § 1-1001.14(b)(3), targets paying the SIGNER (the voter), not the circulator's pay structure: it is a crime to (A) sign or promise to sign / decline to sign a petition \"For any consideration, compensation, gratuity, reward or thing of value,\" or (B) to pay/offer to pay \"any person to

voterFile.cost: Varies — no flat statutory/regulatory price published. The list is provided in response to a written "data request" processed as a D.C. FOIA request (3 DCMR § 510.1), so the charge is the FOIA duplication/processing cost for producing the electronic file. For a single statewide electronic file this is typically nominal (DC FOIA permits only reasonable search/duplication fees and routinely waives small amounts); request a quote/fee estimate from DCBOE to confirm.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — "any person." 3 DCMR § 510.1: "Upon written request, the Board shall provide to any person a list of the registered qualified electors of the District of Columbia or any ward, precinct or ANC SMD therein." There is no candidate/committee or political-use eligibility gate for the standard public list.
voterFile.useRestrictions: No general political-use-only or anti-solicitation restriction is imposed on the public list by 3 DCMR § 510 (access is via FOIA to "any person"). The released record excludes confidential fields — § 510.5 makes social security numbers, dates of birth, email/phone, the registering agency, and any court-/Address-Confidentiality-protected address non-public. A voter's signature image "may be viewed by the public but may not be copied or traced except by Board officials for election administration purposes" (§ 510.8). Inactive registrants' names "shall not be sold by the Board ... except upon specific request of the purchaser and the fact that the registrations are designated as inactive is made known to the purchaser" (D.C. Code § 1-1001.07(j)(5)(D)).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the DC Board of Elections (DCBOE), processed under the D.C. Freedom of Information Act (3 DCMR § 510.1; DCMR Title 3 Chapter 20). No notarized affidavit or political-use sworn statement is required for the standard public list; not an instant self-serve online download. The Board may furnish selective lists (by party, ward, precinct, ANC SMD, voter history, etc.) per § 510.2 and will produce it on electronic/magnetic media per § 510.3.

verification.riskNote: Citizen initiative power exists under Title IV of the Home Rule Act and is implemented by D.C. Code § 1-1001.16. The governing threshold is 5% of citywide registered qualified electors PLUS a ward-distribution requirement of 5% in each of at least 5 of the 8 wards. Numbers recompute each cycle off \"the latest official count of registered qualified electors made by the Board 30 days prior to the submission of the signatures\" (§ 1-1001.16(i)(2)). citations.quote: In order for any initiative measure or referendum measure to qualify for the ballot for consideration by the electors of the District, the proposer of the initiative measure or referendum measure shall secure the valid signatures of registered qualified electors upon the initiative or referendum measure equal in number to 5% of the registered qualified electors in the District; provided, that the total signatures submitted include 5% of the registered qualified electors in each of 5 or more of the 8 wards.

The number of registered qualified electors that is used for computing the requirements described in paragraph (1) of this subsection shall be consistent with the latest official count of registered qualified electors made by the Board 30 days prior to the submission of the signatures for the particular initiative or referendum petition.
D.C. BOARD OF ELECTIONS MONTHLY REPORT OF VOTER REGISTRATION STATISTICS CITYWIDE REGISTRATION SUMMARY AS OF JANUARY 31, 2026 ... TOTALS 359,123 24,497 4,511 85,006 3,086 476,223

A proposer of an initiative measure shall have 180 calendar days, beginning on the 1st calendar day immediately following the date upon which the Board certifies, according to subsection (f) of this section, that the petition form of such initiative measure is in its final form to secure the proper number of valid signatures needed on the initiative petition to qualify such a measure for the ballot, pursuant to subsection (i) of this section and to file such petition with the Board.

A proposer of a referendum measure shall secure the proper number of valid signatures needed on the referendum petition to qualify such a measure for the ballot pursuant to subsection (i) of this section, and shall file such petition with the Board before the act, or part thereof, which is the subject of the referendum has become law according to the provisions of §§ 1-204.04 and 1-206.02(c).

The term "qualified petition circulator" means a person who is at least 17 years of age and who will be 18 years of age on or before the next general election and either: (A) A District resident; or (B) A resident of another jurisdiction who has registered with the Board as a petition circulator and consented to being subject to the subpoena power of the Board and the jurisdiction of the Superior Court of the District of Columbia for the enforcement of subpoenas without respect to the individual's place of residence.

Any person who: (A) For any consideration, compensation, gratuity, reward or thing of value or promise thereof, signs or promises to sign or declines to sign, or promises not to sign any initiative, referendum, or recall petition; or (B) pays or offers or promises to pay, or gives or offers or promises to give any consideration, compensation, gratuity, reward, or thing of value to any person to induce the person to sign or not to sign, the person's signatures upon any initiative, referendum, or recall petition ... shall be fined not more than $10,000 or be imprisoned not more than 1 year, or both.

The term "initiative" means the process by which the electors of the District of Columbia may propose laws (except laws appropriating funds) and present such proposed laws directly to the registered qualified electors of the District of Columbia for their approval or disapproval.

Upon receipt of each proposed initiative or referendum measure, the Board shall refuse to accept the measure if the Board finds that it is not a proper subject of initiative or referendum ... or ... (D) The measure presented would negate or limit an act of the Council of the District of Columbia pursuant to § 1-204.46.

signatures.headline: Florida Constitution Art. XI, sec. 3: petition "signed by a number of electors in each of one half of the congressional districts of the state, and of the state as a whole, equal to eight percent of the votes cast in each of such districts respectively and in the state as a whole in the last preceding election in which presidential electors were chosen." Base = votes cast in the 2024 general (presidential) election: statewide 11,000,768.

XI, sec. 5(b): "shall be submitted to the electors at the general election provided the initiative petition is filed with the custodian of state records no later than February 1 of the year in which the general election is held." Statute 100.371(1)(a): filed with the Secretary of State "no later than February 1 of the year the general election is held" — and a

rules.value: Florida Constitution Art. XI, sec. 3: petition "signed by a number of electors in each of one half of the congressional districts of the state, and of the state as a whole, equal to eight percent of the votes cast in each of such districts respectively and in the state as a whole in the last preceding election in which presidential electors were chosen." Base = votes cast in the 2024 general (presidential) election: statewide 11,000,768.

Constitution Art. XI, sec. 5(b): "shall be submitted to the electors at the general election provided the initiative petition is filed with the custodian of state records no later than February 1 of the year in which the general election is held." Statute 100.371(1)(a): filed with the Secretary of State "no later than February 1 of the year the general election is held" — and a

In at least one half of Florida's congressional districts. Florida has 28 congressional districts, so signatures meeting the 8%-of-district threshold are required in at least 14 of the 28 districts. Per-district 8% thresholds (2024 base, per the Division of Elections 2026 table) range from 23,949 (CD-24) to 37,769 (CD-17); e.g., CD-1 = 34,050, CD-10 = 24,382, CD-17 = 37,769.

Constitution Art. XI, sec. 3: an initiative "except for those limiting the power of government to raise revenue, shall embrace but one subject and matter directly connected therewith." (Single-subject rule applies; the only carve-out is amendments limiting government's power to raise revenue.)

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. See above.

voterFile.cost: Free — "There is no fee associated with this complimentary disk." The statewide monthly voter extract is provided at no charge by the Division of Elections. (An off-cycle/late request is processed as a public records request and "subject to administrative fees for processing.")
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — voter registration and voting history are public record under Florida law; the extract is "publicly available." No eligibility gate (need not be a candidate/committee/party); no political-use-only restriction on this file.
voterFile.useRestrictions: No commercial-/political-use restriction is imposed on the extract itself — the data is public record. The statutory exemptions (s. 97.0585, Fla. Stat.) carve OUT, and prohibit release of, SSN, driver-license/state-ID number, the source of the registration application (voter-registration use only), the signature image (viewable but not copyable), and records of Address Confidentiality Program participants / high-risk professionals. No anti-solicitation penalty clause specific to this file was found on the official pages.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request by email (VoterExtract address) or mail to the Division of Elections; must include contact info, phone/email, and a mailing address. Delivered as a monthly extract on disk by mail (or pick-up). Must be received >=24 hours before the scheduled monthly generation date; a one-time annual standing request can cover all monthly extracts for the calendar year. Not an instant online download, and this contact request "IS NOT available by email or online" for the address detail.

verification.riskNote: Florida citizen initiative = constitutional amendment only (direct). Current-cycle (2024 presidential base) statewide requirement: 880,062 valid signatures = 8% of 11,000,768 votes cast in the 2024 general election (official Division of Elections figure). Distribution: 8% of votes cast in each of at least 14 of Florida's 28 congressional districts. Deadline: petition fully verified/certified by Feb 1 of the general-election year (Art. XI, sec. 5(b); 100.371(1)(a)) — the Feb 1

citations.quote: SECTION 3. Initiative.—The power to propose the revision or amendment of any portion or portions of this constitution by initiative is reserved to the people, provided that, any such revision or amendment, except for those limiting the power of government to raise revenue, shall embrace but one subject and matter directly connected therewith. It may be invoked by filing with the custodian of state records a petition containing a copy of the proposed revision or amendment, signed by a number of electors in each of one half of the congressional districts of the state, and of the state as a whole, equal to eight percent of the votes cast in each of such districts respectively and in the state as a whole in the last preceding election in which presidential electors were chosen.

(b) A proposed amendment or revision of this constitution, or any part of it, by initiative shall be submitted to the electors at the general election provided the initiative petition is filed with the custodian of state records no later than February 1 of the year in which the general election is held.

Constitutional amendments proposed by initiative shall be placed on the ballot for the general election, provided the initiative petition has been filed with the Secretary of State no later than February 1 of the year the general election is held. A petition shall be deemed to be filed with the Secretary of State upon the date the secretary determines that valid and verified petition forms have been signed by the constitutionally required number and distribution of voters under this code.

In addition to other requirements, a sponsor must obtain valid petitions signed by a number of Florida voters in each of one half of the congressional districts of the state, and of the state as a whole, equal to eight percent of the votes cast in each of such districts respectively and in the state as a whole in the last preceding election in which presidential electors were chosen.

A sponsor may not compensate a petition circulator based on the number of petition forms gathered or the time within which a number of petition forms are gathered. This prohibition includes, but is not limited to, paying a specified amount per petition form gathered, basing an hourly rate on the number of petition forms gathered over a specified period of time, or providing any other benefit or form of compensation based on the number of petition forms gathered.

A person may not collect signatures or initiative petitions if he or she: 1. Has been convicted of a felony violation and has not had his or her right to vote restored. 2. Is not a citizen of the United States. 3. Is not a resident of this state.

Proposed amendments to the Florida Constitution may be made proposed by a legislative joint resolution, a statewide initiative petition, or a proposal from the Constitution Revision Commission or the Taxation and Budget Reform Commission. A proposed amendment requires at least 60% approval from voters to pass [see Florida Constitution, Article XI, Fla. Const.].
The sponsor of an initiative amendment may not sponsor more than one amendment and must, before circulating any petition forms, register as a political committee pursuant to s. 106.03

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature pay for initiatives is ALLOWED in Idaho. Idaho formerly banned paying circulators on a per-signature basis, but a federal court struck that ban down in 2001 (Idaho Coalition United for Bears v. Cenarrusa). Per Idaho Code 34-1807, circulators must be Idaho residents at least 18 years of age and must personally witness each signature (signatures are void otherwise).

voterFile.cost: $20 flat per report for the statewide "Voter Registration Information" list (or $20 for "Voter Registration AND Voter History"); Absentee Ballot Reports are $10. Verbatim: "Voter Registration Information ($20/report)", "Voter Registration AND Voter History ($20/report)", "Absentee Ballot Report ($10/report)". A separate generic public-records copy fee of 25¢/page applies to non-voter records, not to these reports.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Effectively open to the public / any requesting party — the form has no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate. The requester must agree to the Idaho Code 34-437A(3) use restriction and certify the request under penalty (67-6624). The voter file may be used "for any political purpose," so campaigns/committees clearly qualify. The form itself notes the requester's submitted info "will become public record."
voterFile.useRestrictions: Anti-solicitation restriction applies. Per Idaho Code 34-437A(3) (quoted on the form): the list may not be used to mail/deliver any advertisement, offer, or solicitation for money/services/anything of value — i.e., no commercial mailing or telephone list use (also referenced via Idaho Code 74-120 on the public-records page). However, the statute expressly carves out political use: "any such list and label may be used for any political purpose," which covers a campaign verifying petition signatures against registered voters. Requester signs a certification under Section 67-6624, Idaho Code, making misuse a sworn-statement violation.
voterFile.requestProcess: Online request form (no instant download; an SOS staff-fulfilled report). Submit the "Voter Registration and Absentee Ballot Reports" form at forms.sos.idaho.gov: choose scope (Statewide / Congressional District / Legislative District), report type (Voter Registration Information, or Voter Registration AND Voter History), then provide a signed Certification ("Yes, I agree" under Section 67-6624, Idaho Code) plus a printed name/signature and acknowledge the 34-437A(3) use prohibition. The report is delivered as a .CSV download link emailed to the requester. ("Reports requested online will be provided in .CSV text format and made available as a download link sent to the email address you provide above.")

citations.quote: The people reserve to themselves the power to propose laws, and enact the same at the polls independent of the legislature. This power is known as the initiative, and legal voters may, under such conditions and in such manner as may be provided by acts of the legislature, initiate any desired legislation and cause the same to be submitted to the vote of the people at a general election for their approval or rejection.

Citizens of Idaho may only initiate legislation as a state statute. Idahoans may also repeal legislation via veto referendum, but cannot place a constitutional amendment on the ballot via initiative. The Idaho State Legislature, on the other hand, may place measures on the ballot as legislatively referred constitutional amendments.

Pursuant to Section 34-1805, Idaho Code, there shall be affixed to the petition signatures of legal voters equal in number to not less than six percent ( 6%) of the qualified electors at the last general election in each of at least 18 Legislative Districts; provided, however, the total number of signatures shall be equal to or greater than six percent ( 6%) of the qualified electors of the state at the time of the last general election. Each signature page shall be signed by electors from one county only.

The sponsors of a measure must collect signatures from six percent of the total registered voters at the time of the last general election in order to qualify for the ballot. The submitted voter signatures must meet the requirements for distribution across at least 18 legislative districts, with at least 6 percent of registered voters from each district by the deadline.

signatures.headline: Art. XIV, Sec. 3: 'Amendments to Article IV of this Constitution may be proposed by a petition signed by a number of electors equal in number to at least eight percent of the total votes cast for candidates for Governor in the preceding gubernatorial election.' (corroborated by 10 ILCS 5/28-9: 'at least 8% of the total votes cast for candidates for Governor in the preceding gubernatorial election.') Base = 2022 Illinois gubernatorial election, 4,104,636 votes cast for Governo

deadline.headline: Art. XIV, Sec. 3 (verbatim): petition 'shall be filed with the Secretary of State at least six months before that general election.' Additionally the petition 'shall have been signed by the petitioning electors not more than twenty-four months preceding that general election.' For the Nov. 3, 2026 general election the filing deadline is May 3, 2026 (Ballotpedia corroboration).

XIV, Sec. 3: 'Amendments to Article IV of this Constitution may be proposed by a petition signed by a number of electors equal in number to at least eight percent of the total votes cast for candidates for Governor in the preceding gubernatorial election.' rules.note: direct/indirect: direct (goes straight to the voters at the general election; the General Assembly has no role in approving or rejecting an initiated amendment)

rules.value: Art. XIV, Sec. 3 (verbatim): petition 'shall be filed with the Secretary of State at least six months before that general election.' Additionally the petition 'shall have been signed by the petitioning electors not more than twenty-four months preceding that general election.' For the Nov. 3, 2026 general election the filing deadline is May 3, 2026 (Ballotpedia corroboration).
none (the constitution and 10 ILCS 5/28-9 impose no geographic/county/district distribution requirement)

Subject-matter limited, not a conventional single-subject rule: 'Amendments shall be limited to structural and procedural subjects contained in Article IV.' (Art. XIV, Sec. 3). Citizen initiative may amend ONLY Article IV (the Legislature article), and only as to structural and procedural subjects within it.

compensation.operatingStance: The Illinois Election Code does not address or prohibit per-signature compensation of circulators for the constitutional initiative; there is no pay-per-signature ban and no spreading ban. Illinois is NOT among the ~10 states (AZ, AR, FL, MT, ND, OK, OR, SD, UT, WY per Ballotpedia) that ban per-signature pay for initiatives. Circulator requirements are minimal: under 10 ILCS 5/

voterFile.cost: $500 flat for the entire-state file (committee version). Sub-state files are cheaper per the published schedule (e.g., Congressional District $75, Senate District $30, Representative District $25, Cook County / City of Chicago $100 each; "Others" $25 minimum). The redacted PUBLIC (non-committee) file is instead priced at statutory "cost of duplication plus a 15% fee for administration." Paid by electronic funds transfer (from the committee's own account); no application is processed without payment.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: For the full (unredacted, with-residential-address) file: registered State or local political committees, for bona fide political purposes (and governmental entities for a governmental purpose). The general public can only obtain a redacted version (street numbers, DOB, SSN, phone, partial email removed) and only for the limited purposes in the National Voter Registration Act, 52 U.S.C. 20507(i). The full statewide list may not be disclosed to anyone other than a State/local political committee or a governmental entity.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-use-only. Data "shall be used only for bona fide political purposes" and "shall not be used under any circumstances for purposes of commercial solicitation or other business purposes." The application warns misuse "may result in substantial penalty, including, but not limited to, conviction of a class 4 felony (10 ILCS 5/4-8, 5-7, 6-35)." By statute (10 ILCS 5/1A-25(c)) records may not be used for any personal, private, or commercial purpose, including intimidation/threat/deception or advertising/solicitation/sale/marketing; SBE must deny requests from anyone under a court order finding a violation. Verifying petition signatures (matching signers to registered voters) is a bona fide political purpose for a committee.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written application: download the SBE "Request for Voter Information" form (Revised 07-07-2025), complete it, and submit by email to bpeterson@elections.il.gov for review; SBE then provides an electronic-payment link and the file is run after payment. Political committees must check a verification box and have the candidate, treasurer, or chair sign a sworn statement of permitted (bona fide political) use. Files are delivered encrypted and password-protected, with the password given only to the original requestor. This is a request-and-approval process, not an instant download.

citations.field: signatureBasis / signatureRequirement / deadline / distribution / singleSubject (primary constitutional source)
citations.quote: Text of Section 3: Constitutional Initiative for Legislative Article. Amendments to Article IV of this Constitution may be proposed by a petition signed by a number of electors equal in number to at least eight percent of the total votes cast for candidates for Governor in the preceding gubernatorial election. Amendments shall be limited to structural and procedural subjects contained in Article IV. A petition shall contain the text of the proposed amendment and the date of the general election at which the proposed amendment is to be submitted, shall have been signed by the petitioning electors not more than twenty-four months preceding that general election and shall be filed with the Secretary of State at least six months before that general election.

Sec. 28-9. Petitions for proposed amendments to Article IV of the Constitution pursuant to Section 3, Article XIV of the Constitution shall be signed by a number of electors equal in number to at least 8% of the total votes cast for candidates for Governor in the preceding gubernatorial election. Such petition shall have been signed by the petitioning electors not more than 24 months preceding the general election at which the proposed amendment is to be submitted and shall be filed with the Secretary of State at least 6 months before that general election.

In Illinois, the signature requirement for initiated constitutional amendments is 8 percent of the total votes cast for governor in the preceding gubernatorial election. ... | 2026 | 328,371 | ... | 2022 | 4,104,636 | ... The deadline for submitting signatures to get an initiative or referendum on the ballot for a particular general election is six months before that election. | 2026 | May 3, 2026 |
In Illinois, citizens can initiate constitutional amendments that amend Article IV of the Illinois Constitution.

At the bottom of each sheet of such petition shall be added a circulator's statement, signed by a person 18 years of age or older who is a citizen of the United States, stating the street address or rural route number, as the case may be, as well as the county, city, village or town, and state; certifying that the signatures on that sheet of the petition were signed in his or her presence and are genuine... In addition to the foregoing requirements, a petition proposing an amendment to Article IV of the Constitution pursuant to Section 3 of Article XIV of the Constitution ... shall be in conformity with the requirements of Section 28-9 of this Article.

signatures.headline: 3 percent of votes cast for governor in the most recent gubernatorial election, excluding blanks (Article XLVIII). NO second signature round; instead the amendment must be approved by at least one-quarter (25%) of all members of the Legislature in two consecutive joint sessions (consecutive General Courts) before reaching the ballot.

deadline.headline: Signatures due first Wednesday in December (filed with local registrars two weeks prior, ~Nov. 19, 2025). For petitions certified by the AG in 2025, the earliest possible ballot is November 2028, because "constitutional amendments proposed by initiative petition require approval by at least 25 percent of two consecutive joint sessions of the Legislature."

rules.value: 3 percent of votes cast for governor in the most recent gubernatorial election, excluding blanks (Article XLVIII). NO second signature round; instead the amendment must be approved by at least one-quarter (25%) of all members of the Legislature in two consecutive joint sessions (consecutive General Courts) before reaching the ballot.
rules.note: direct/indirect: indirect: requires approval by 25% of legislators in two consecutive joint sessions in addition to the signature step; minimum ~2-3 years from signature submission to ballot

rules.value: Signatures due first Wednesday in December (filed with local registrars two weeks prior, ~Nov. 19, 2025). For petitions certified by the AG in 2025, the earliest possible ballot is November 2028, because "constitutional amendments proposed by initiative petition require approval by at least 25 percent of two consecutive joint sessions of the Legislature."
No more than one-quarter of the certified signatures on any petition may come from any one county (Article XLVIII, General Provisions, Part II).
Petition must "contain only subjects which are related or which are mutually dependent" (Article XLVIII).

compensation.operatingStance: Massachusetts ALLOWS paying signature gatherers on a per-signature (pay-per-signature) basis (Ballotpedia: "Massachusetts allows paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected"). Massachusetts is NOT among the ~10 states (AZ, AR, FL, MT, ND, OK, OR, SD, UT, WY per Ballotpedia) that ban per-signature pay for initiatives. Multiple bills to ban pay-per-sign

voterFile.cost: State voter file: no published flat price — "a fair and reasonable cost not to exceed the cost of printing or preparing computer readable documents" (M.G.L. c.51 §47C). Municipal route (for non-qualifying candidates): each city/town charges only "the cost of producing the record" — in practice a few dollars per local street list/book (e.g., $2–$20 per town).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: The statewide central registry of voters is NOT a public record and is restricted by statute to: state party committees, statewide candidate committees, state ballot question committees, the jury commissioner, the adjutant general, and any other entity the Secretary designates by regulation. The SOS describes this as "only available to statewide campaigns and political parties." Candidates who do not qualify (e.g., district/local candidates) must instead obtain the public municipal voter lists from each city/town election office; those local lists are available to the general public under the Public Records Law.
voterFile.useRestrictions: State voter file is provided only under a licensing agreement that "prohibits them from sharing or selling the information," and the names/addresses "shall not be a matter of public record." The file excludes non-public fields (phone, email, veteran status, permanent-absentee status, confidential voters, minors). Confidential voters (court order / shelter / Address Confidentiality Program) are entirely omitted. The public municipal lists carry the standard MA public-records use norms but are openly inspectable.
voterFile.requestProcess: State voter file: written request to the Secretary of the Commonwealth's Elections Division plus a mandatory signed licensing agreement that prohibits sharing or selling the data (eligibility-gated). Municipal route: written/in-person public-records request to each local election office (town/city clerk or board of registrars) for the public voter list, payable at cost of production.

verification.riskNote: Current-cycle valid-signature figures for the November 2026 ballot are taken directly from the Massachusetts Attorney General's official press release (9/03/2025): initiated statute = 74,574 first-round signatures due Dec. 3, 2025, plus 12,429 second-round signatures due July 1, 2026; these match Ballotpedia's 2026 table exactly. Base = 2022 gubernatorial election (3% = 74,574 implies a base of ~2,485,800 governor votes; 0.5% = 12,429). Veto referendum 37,245 (1.5%) is from B

citations.quote: Proponents of a proposed law that was certified may now gather and file with the Secretary of the Commonwealth the signatures of 74,574 registered voters by December 3, 2025. Once these signatures are verified, the proposal will be sent to the State Legislature in January 2026 for its consideration on or before the first Wednesday of May 2026. If the Legislature does not enact a proposal, proponents must gather 12,429 additional signatures from registered voters by July 1, 2026, to place the proposed law on the November 2026 ballot.

The process for proposed constitutional amendments is different, as constitutional amendments proposed by initiative petition require approval by at least 25 percent of two consecutive joint sessions of the Legislature. Accordingly, no such proposal certified today can appear on the ballot before November 2028.

The Massachusetts Constitution requires that proposed initiatives be in the proper form for submission to voters, not be substantially the same as any measure qualified to appear on the ballot in either of the two preceding statewide elections, contain only subjects that are related to each other or mutually dependent, and not involve a set of issues that are specifically excluded from the ballot initiative process by the Massachusetts Constitution. For example, a petition cannot be certified if it relates to religion, religious practices or religious institutions; the powers, creation or abolition of the courts; the appointment, compensation or tenure of judges; a specific appropriation of funds from the state treasury; or if it infringes on certain other constitutional rights listed in Article 48, such as trial by jury, freedom of the press, freedom of speech, and free elections.

For an amendment or statute, submitted signatures must equal 3 percent of votes cast for governor in the most recent gubernatorial election, excluding blanks. If the legislature declines to act on a proposed statute, supporters are required to collect a second round of signatures totaling 0.5 percent of the votes last cast for governor, excluding blanks. For proposed amendments, one-quarter of the legislature must approve the petition in a joint session—a second round of signatures is not required and does not overrule rejection by more than three-quarters of the legislature.

For a veto referendum, signatures must equal 1.5 percent of the total votes cast for governor in the most recent gubernatorial election. If the petitioners submit a written request for the suspension of the targeted law prior to the referendum election, signatures equal to 2 percent of the total votes last cast for governor are required to both suspend the law and force an election.
No more than one-quarter (18,644) of the certified signatures on the initiative or referendum petitions may come from any one county.
Massachusetts allows paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.
Massachusetts does not require petition circulators to be state residents.

compensation.operatingStance: Massachusetts ALLOWS paying signature gatherers on a per-signature (pay-per-signature) basis (Ballotpedia: "Massachusetts allows paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected"). voterFile.cost: State voter file: no published flat price — "a fair and reasonable cost not to exceed the cost of printing or preparing computer readable documents" (M.G.L. c.51 §47C). Municipal route (for non-qualifying candidates): each city/town charges only "the cost of producing the record" — in practice a few dollars per local street list/book (e.g., $2–$20 per town).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: The statewide central registry of voters is NOT a public record and is restricted by statute to: state party committees, statewide candidate committees, state ballot question committees, the jury commissioner, the adjutant general, and any other entity the Secretary designates by regulation. The SOS describes this as "only available to statewide campaigns and political parties." Candidates who do not qualify (e.g., district/local candidates) must instead obtain the public municipal voter lists from each city/town election office; those local lists are available to the general public under the Public Records Law.
voterFile.useRestrictions: State voter file is provided only under a licensing agreement that "prohibits them from sharing or selling the information," and the names/addresses "shall not be a matter of public record." The file excludes non-public fields (phone, email, veteran status, permanent-absentee status, confidential voters, minors). Confidential voters (court order / shelter / Address Confidentiality Program) are entirely omitted. The public municipal lists carry the standard MA public-records use norms but are openly inspectable.
voterFile.requestProcess: State voter file: written request to the Secretary of the Commonwealth's Elections Division plus a mandatory signed licensing agreement that prohibits sharing or selling the data (eligibility-gated). Municipal route: written/in-person public-records request to each local election office (town/city clerk or board of registrars) for the public voter list, payable at cost of production.
