none
none — Maine has no single-subject rule for initiatives. The Secretary of State's pre-circulation review under 21-A M.R.S. §901(3-A) addresses only drafting form/conventions, not subject matter.

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature / paid circulation is PERMITTED in Maine. Maine is NOT among the ~10 states that ban per-signature pay for initiatives (AZ, AR, FL, MT, ND, OK, OR, SD, UT, WY per Ballotpedia). 21-A M.R.S. §903-A(5) expressly contemplates paid circulators, requiring only a disclosure: 'A person or organization that pays individuals to circulate petitions, other than by reimbursing

voterFile.cost: Electronic file priced by record count (tiered): $11 for 1-1,000 records up to $2,200 for 900,001+ records. A full statewide file (Maine has ~1M+ registered voters) falls in the top tier = $2,200. Printed/PDF report: $1 first page + 25 cents/additional page ($0.75/page for mailing labels). Up to 11 free updates in the 12 months after purchase.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated and political-use-only. The Party/Campaign Use Voter File (21-A MRS §196-A(1)(B)) is available to parties, candidates, issue campaigns, GOTV efforts directly related to a campaign, and currently-serving elected/appointed officials. The requestor must submit proof they are authorized by the candidate, party, or issue campaign (e.g., an authorization letter). Members of the public may obtain the same file only via a written request to the Secretary of State for a purpose protected by the NVRA. Default CVR data is otherwise confidential, accessible only by state/municipal election officials.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strict. Information from the CVR may not be used for any commercial purpose or any solicitation not directly related to political-party/campaign/GOTV activities (or NVRA-protected purposes); selling or distributing the data to others for commercial use is prohibited; officials may not transfer their copy to another entity. Discrimination on the basis of disability, race, color, age, sex, sexual orientation, religion, ancestry, or national origin using the data is prohibited. Civil penalties: up to $1,000 for a first offense and up to $5,000 for each subsequent offense (21-A MRS §196-A, sub-§5).
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form + payment + proof of authorization. The "Request for Obtaining Data from Maine CVR" form must be typed or completed in ink, signed, and submitted to the Secretary of State (Division of Elections) by mail (CVR Manager, 184 State House Station, Augusta, ME 04333-0184), fax (287-6545), or scanned email to cec.elections@maine.gov (must contain an actual handwritten signature). Campaign requestors must attach written proof of authorization from the candidate/party/campaign. Fees must be paid before data release. Office responds within 5 business days. Electronic data is delivered on physical media (thumb drive); email transfer of the data is not permitted.

Circulator payment. A person or organization that pays individuals to circulate petitions, other than by reimbursing travel expenses, shall provide a list of those individuals to the Secretary of State along with information indicating the method by which the individuals are being compensated when the petitions are filed pursuant to the Constitution of Maine, Article IV, Part Third, Section 17 or 18.

citations.field: process / measure types (direct initiative + people's veto)
citations.quote: To initiate proceedings for a people's veto referendum or the direct initiative of legislation, provided in the Constitution of Maine, Article IV, Part Third, Sections 17 and 18, a voter shall submit a written application to the Department of the Secretary of State on a form designed by the Secretary of State.

An application for a people's veto referendum petition must be filed in the Department of the Secretary of State within 10 business days after adjournment of the legislative session at which the Act in question was passed. A direct initiative of legislation must meet the filing deadlines specified in the Constitution of Maine, Article IV, Part Third, Section 18.

The Secretary of State shall review the proposed law for a direct initiative of legislation within 15 business days after receipt of the application and either reject the application or provide a first revised draft of the initiative legislation to the applicant within that time. The Secretary of State may reject the application if the Secretary of State determines that the proposed law: A. Does not conform to the form prescribed by the Secretary of State; or B. Does not conform to the essential aspects of the drafting conventions established for the Maine Revised Statutes.

The circulator of a petition must sign the petition and verify by oath or affirmation before a notary public or other person authorized by law to administer oaths or affirmations that the circulator personally witnessed all of the signatures to the petition and that to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief each signature is the signature of the person whose name it purports to be

citations.field: process / measure types (direct initiative + people's veto)
citations.quote: To initiate proceedings for a people's veto referendum or the direct initiative of legislation, provided in the Constitution of Maine, Article IV, Part Third, Sections 17 and 18, a voter shall submit a written application to the Department of the Secretary of State on a form designed by the Secretary of State.

An application for a people's veto referendum petition must be filed in the Department of the Secretary of State within 10 business days after adjournment of the legislative session at which the Act in question was passed. A direct initiative of legislation must meet the filing deadlines specified in the Constitution of Maine, Article IV, Part Third, Section 18.

The Secretary of State shall review the proposed law for a direct initiative of legislation within 15 business days after receipt of the application and either reject the application or provide a first revised draft of the initiative legislation to the applicant within that time. The Secretary of State may reject the application if the Secretary of State determines that the proposed law: A. Does not conform to the form prescribed by the Secretary of State; or B. Does not conform to the essential aspects of the drafting conventions established for the Maine Revised Statutes.

voterFile.cost: No published flat statewide price; varies — request quote. By statute the requester pays "the cost of making, certifying, and delivering the tape, disk, or listing" (MCL 168.522); when obtained from the state via FOIA, the Dept. of State "may charge a fee to cover the cost of complying" (labor + media). Note: the separate $15/record "Record sales" program is for DRIVER/VEHICLE records only, NOT voter data.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Broadly available — the statute requires the file be delivered "to any person, upon request" (MCL 168.522). Not gated to candidates/committees/parties; the statewide Qualified Voter File data is obtained from the Bureau of Elections via written/FOIA request (it is NOT sold through the SOS commercial "Record sales / List sales" program, which covers only driver and vehicle records).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Statutorily protected/exempt fields are stripped from any released list — no driver-license/state-ID number, no month/day of birth, no phone number, no last-4 SSN, and no digitized signature (MCL 168.509gg; MCL 168.522(2) bars these from the list, though it may include year of birth). For the SOS's separate bulk data programs the state warns data "cannot be used for marketing or solicitation purposes... violations can be prosecuted as felony infractions."
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Michigan Department of State / Bureau of Elections (election-data FOIA request). FOIA requests "must be submitted in writing" — online via MiLogin, or by mail/email to the MDOS FOIA Coordinator. (Local lists may alternatively be requested from a city/township/village clerk under MCL 168.522.) No instant online public download of the statewide file.

citations.quote: Every petition shall include the full text of the proposed amendment, and be signed by registered electors of the state equal in number to at least 10 percent of the total vote cast for all candidates for governor at the last preceding general election at which a governor was elected.
Such petitions shall be filed with the person authorized by law to receive the same at least 120 days before the election at which the proposed amendment is to be voted upon.

To invoke the initiative or referendum, petitions signed by a number of registered electors, not less than eight percent for initiative and five percent for referendum of the total vote cast for all candidates for governor at the last preceding general election at which a governor was elected shall be required.

Any law proposed by initiative petition shall be either enacted or rejected by the legislature without change or amendment within 40 session days from the time such petition is received by the legislature.

The power of referendum does not extend to acts making appropriations for state institutions or to meet deficiencies in state funds and must be invoked in the manner prescribed by law within 90 days following the final adjournment of the legislative session at which the law was enacted.

Single-subject rule See also: Single-subject rule Michigan does not have a single-subject rule for ballot initiatives.

On January 24, 2022, the Michigan Supreme Court ruled that the distribution requirement was unconstitutional. Justice Megan Cavanagh said, "It would run directly contrary to the clear intention that nothing more than a minimum number of signatures from the statewide population is necessary to propose changes to Michigan's laws." The court also overturned a requirement that paid signature gatherers register with the state but said requiring paid circulators to identify that they are paid on their petitions forms is constitutional.
Michigan does not require circulators to be residents of the state. Circulators who are not residents of the state must indicate this on their circulator certificate.
Pay-per-signature See also: Pay-per-signature Michigan allows paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.

In Michigan, petitioners have 180 days to collect the required signatures for an initiated statute or an initiated constitutional amendment. Signatures older than 180 days at the time of filing are considered invalid. In addition, any signatures collected before a November election where a governor is elected, cannot be submitted after that election. Amendment petitions must be filed 120 days prior to the election. Petitions for statutes must be filed 160 days prior to the election, allowing the legislators 40 days to pass the proposed law.

Base per Art. III, Sec. 53: "The total vote for governor at the general election last preceding the filing of any initiative or referendum petition shall be used to determine the number of legal voters necessary to sign the petition." Base year = 2024 gubernatorial

SoS published 2026-cycle deadline: "the submission deadline for signatures is 5:00 PM on May 3" / "5:00 PM on May 3, 2026" (all pages submitted at one time, organized by county).

8% of the 2024 gubernatorial vote in each of two-thirds (6 of 8) of Missouri's congressional districts. SoS: "a petition must have a minimum number of signatures in six of eight congressional districts." Per-district 8% requirements (2024 votes cast): CD1 24,954; CD2 34,512; CD3 32,099; CD4 29,670; CD5 26,720; CD6 30,247; CD7 29,758; CD8 28,866. The 170,215 statewide minimum is

Mo. Const. Art. III, Sec. 50: "Petitions for constitutional amendments shall not contain more than one amended and revised article of this constitution, or one new article which shall not contain more than one subject and matters properly connected therewith." Enacting clause must be: "Be it resolved by the people of the state of Missouri that the Constitution be amended:"

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature pay to CIRCULATORS is permitted/unaddressed-as-a-ban — paid circulation is expressly contemplated. The statutory circulator's affidavit (RSMo 116.030 referendum / 116.040 initiative) requires the circulator to disclose: "I am at least 18 years of age. I do .... do not .... (check one) expect to be paid for circulating this petition. If paid, list the payer .......

voterFile.cost: Statutory fee set under Chapter 610 (Sunshine Law cost-recovery), determined by the Secretary of State / local election authority — not published as a fixed online price (varies — request quote). Ballotpedia reports the statewide file cost as "$35" (secondary source, unverified against an official price list).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Not the general public. The statewide file is restricted to a candidate, a duly authorized representative of a campaign committee, or a political party committee (terms defined in RSMo 130.011). RSMo 115.157.1 bars furnishing any registration info "to any member of the public" except as the section provides; in even-numbered years (2026 qualifies) each election authority must, upon request, supply its jurisdiction's voter registration list "to all candidates and party committees."
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-use-only. The master voter registration list "shall not be used for commercial purposes" (RSMo 115.158.6) — defined to include resale or obtaining names/addresses for solicitation — and "nothing in this chapter shall require such voter information to be released to the public over the internet" (115.157.3). Violation of the statewide-system use restriction is a class B misdemeanor (115.158.6).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Secretary of State (statewide, electronic or printed media, for a fee under RSMo 115.157.4) or to the relevant local election authority/county clerk for its jurisdiction's list (RSMo 115.157.5). Not an instant online download — Missouri voter registration is decentralized across 116 local election jurisdictions, and the file is provided per-request for a fee; no public self-service portal is published. Contact the SOS Elections Division (573-751-2301).

verification.riskNote: All core figures are grounded in primary sources: the Missouri Constitution (Art. III, Secs. 50, 52(a), 53), RSMo Ch. 116 (116.030, 116.080, 116.150), and the Secretary of State's official 2026-cycle materials, which publish the exact validated minimums for this cycle: 170,215 valid signatures for a constitutional amendment and 106,384 for a statute/referendum, based on the 2024 gubernatorial vote, distributed across six of eight congressional districts. The statewide minimum

citations.quote: Initiative petitions proposing amendments to the constitution shall be signed by eight percent of the legal voters in each of two-thirds of the congressional districts in the state, and petitions proposing laws shall be signed by five percent of such voters. Every such petition shall be filed with the secretary of state not less than six months before the election and shall contain an enacting clause and the full text of the measure. Petitions for constitutional amendments shall not contain more than one amended and revised article of this constitution, or one new article which shall not contain more than one subject and matters properly connected therewith, and the enacting clause thereof shall be "Be it resolved by the people of the state of Missouri that the Constitution be amended:". Petitions for laws shall contain not more than one subject which shall be expressed clearly in the title, and the enacting clause thereof shall be "Be it enacted by the people of the state of Missouri:".

The total vote for governor at the general election last preceding the filing of any initiative or referendum petition shall be used to determine the number of legal voters necessary to sign the petition. In submitting the same to the people the secretary of state and all other officers shall be governed by general laws.

A referendum may be ordered (except as to laws necessary for the immediate preservation of the public peace, health or safety, and laws making appropriations for the current expenses of the state government, for the maintenance of state institutions and for the support of public schools) either by petitions signed by five percent of the legal voters in each of two-thirds of the congressional districts in the state, or by the general assembly, as other bills are enacted. Referendum petitions shall be filed with the secretary of state not more than ninety days after the final adjournment of the session of the general assembly which passed the bill on which the referendum is demanded.

CONSTITUTIONAL AMENDMENT 170,215 ... STATUTORY AMENDMENT 106,384 ... MINIMUM NUMBER OF SIGNATURES TO GET ON BALLOT. According to state law, a petition must have a minimum number of signatures in six of eight congressional districts. The minimums are based on the number of votes cast in the last gubernatorial election. ... CONGRESSIONAL DISTRICT / GUBERNATORIAL VOTES CAST IN 2024 / 5% FOR STATUTORY / 8% FOR CONSTITUTIONAL: 1ST 311,915 15,596 24,954; 2ND 431,395 21,570 34,512; 3RD 401,227 20,062 32,099; 4TH 370,875 18,544 29,670; 5TH 333,988 16,700 26,720; 6TH 378,080 18,904 30,247; 7TH 371,969 18,599 29,758; 8TH 360,817 18,041 28,866.

All petitioners must deliver signed petitions to the secretary of state's office no less than six months before the election. For the 2026 election cycle, the submission deadline for signatures is 5:00 PM on May 3. ... For the 2026 election cycle, the number of valid signatures needed is outlined on page 5 (Table A). ... MINIMUM SIGNATURES REQUIRED 106,384 / 170,215.

Each petition circulator shall be at least eighteen years of age and registered with the secretary of state. Signatures collected by any circulator who has not registered with the secretary of state pursuant to this chapter on or before 5:00 p.m. on the final day for filing petitions with the secretary of state shall not be counted. A petition circulator shall be deemed registered at the time such circulator delivers a signed circulator's affidavit pursuant to section 116.030, with respect to a referendum petition, or section 116.040, with respect to an initiative petition, to the office of the secretary of state. No person shall qualify as a petition circulator who has been convicted of, found guilty of, or pled guilty to an offense involving forgery.
I am at least 18 years of age. I do ______ do not ______ (check one) expect to be paid for circulating this petition. If paid, list the payer ______

deadline.headline: Mo. Const. Art. III, Sec. 50: "filed with the secretary of state not less than six months before the election." SoS published 2026-cycle deadline: "5:00 PM on May 3, 2026".

rules.value: Mo. Const. Art. III, Sec. 50: "petitions proposing laws shall be signed by five percent of such voters" (i.e., five percent of the legal voters in each of two-thirds of the congressional districts). Base per Art. III, Sec. 53: total vote for governor at the last preceding general election (2024). Two-thirds of 8 = 6 of 8 districts.
Mo. Const. Art. III, Sec. 50: "filed with the secretary of state not less than six months before the election." SoS published 2026-cycle deadline: "5:00 PM on May 3, 2026".

5% of the 2024 gubernatorial vote in each of 6 of 8 congressional districts ("six of eight congressional districts"). Per-district 5% requirements (2024 votes cast): CD1 15,596; CD2 21,570; CD3 20,062; CD4 18,544; CD5 16,700; CD6 18,904; CD7 18,599; CD8 18,041. The 106,384 statewide minimum is the sum of the six lowest districts (CDs 1,4,5,6,7,8).

Mo. Const. Art. III, Sec. 50: "Petitions for laws shall contain not more than one subject which shall be expressed clearly in the title." Enacting clause must be: "Be it enacted by the people of the state of Missouri:"

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature pay to CIRCULATORS is permitted/unaddressed-as-a-ban — paid circulation is expressly contemplated. The statutory circulator's affidavit (RSMo 116.030 referendum / 116.040 initiative) requires the circulator to disclose: "I am at least 18 years of age. I do .... do not .... (check one) expect to be paid for circulating this petition. If paid, list the payer .......

voterFile.cost: Statutory fee set under Chapter 610 (Sunshine Law cost-recovery), determined by the Secretary of State / local election authority — not published as a fixed online price (varies — request quote). Ballotpedia reports the statewide file cost as "$35" (secondary source, unverified against an official price list).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Not the general public. The statewide file is restricted to a candidate, a duly authorized representative of a campaign committee, or a political party committee (terms defined in RSMo 130.011). RSMo 115.157.1 bars furnishing any registration info "to any member of the public" except as the section provides; in even-numbered years (2026 qualifies) each election authority must, upon request, supply its jurisdiction's voter registration list "to all candidates and party committees."
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-use-only. The master voter registration list "shall not be used for commercial purposes" (RSMo 115.158.6) — defined to include resale or obtaining names/addresses for solicitation — and "nothing in this chapter shall require such voter information to be released to the public over the internet" (115.157.3). Violation of the statewide-system use restriction is a class B misdemeanor (115.158.6).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Secretary of State (statewide, electronic or printed media, for a fee under RSMo 115.157.4) or to the relevant local election authority/county clerk for its jurisdiction's list (RSMo 115.157.5). Not an instant online download — Missouri voter registration is decentralized across 116 local election jurisdictions, and the file is provided per-request for a fee; no public self-service portal is published. Contact the SOS Elections Division (573-751-2301).

III, Secs. 50, 52(a), 53), RSMo Ch. 116 (116.030, 116.080, 116.150), and the Secretary of State's official 2026-cycle materials, which publish the exact validated minimums for this cycle: 170,215 valid signatures for a constitutional amendment and 106,384 for a statute/referendum, based on the 2024 gubernatorial vote, distributed across six of eight congressional districts. The statewide minimum

citations.quote: Initiative petitions proposing amendments to the constitution shall be signed by eight percent of the legal voters in each of two-thirds of the congressional districts in the state, and petitions proposing laws shall be signed by five percent of such voters. Every such petition shall be filed with the secretary of state not less than six months before the election and shall contain an enacting clause and the full text of the measure. Petitions for constitutional amendments shall not contain more than one amended and revised article of this constitution, or one new article which shall not contain more than one subject and matters properly connected therewith, and the enacting clause thereof shall be "Be it resolved by the people of the state of Missouri that the Constitution be amended:". Petitions for laws shall contain not more than one subject which shall be expressed clearly in the title, and the enacting clause thereof shall be "Be it enacted by the people of the state of Missouri:".

The total vote for governor at the general election last preceding the filing of any initiative or referendum petition shall be used to determine the number of legal voters necessary to sign the petition. In submitting the same to the people the secretary of state and all other officers shall be governed by general laws.

A referendum may be ordered (except as to laws necessary for the immediate preservation of the public peace, health or safety, and laws making appropriations for the current expenses of the state government, for the maintenance of state institutions and for the support of public schools) either by petitions signed by five percent of the legal voters in each of two-thirds of the congressional districts in the state, or by the general assembly, as other bills are enacted. Referendum petitions shall be filed with the secretary of state not more than ninety days after the final adjournment of the session of the general assembly which passed the bill on which the referendum is demanded.

CONSTITUTIONAL AMENDMENT 170,215 ... STATUTORY AMENDMENT 106,384 ... MINIMUM NUMBER OF SIGNATURES TO GET ON BALLOT. According to state law, a petition must have a minimum number of signatures in six of eight congressional districts. The minimums are based on the number of votes cast in the last gubernatorial election. ... CONGRESSIONAL DISTRICT / GUBERNATORIAL VOTES CAST IN 2024 / 5% FOR STATUTORY / 8% FOR CONSTITUTIONAL: 1ST 311,915 15,596 24,954; 2ND 431,395 21,570 34,512; 3RD 401,227 20,062 32,099; 4TH 370,875 18,544 29,670; 5TH 333,988 16,700 26,720; 6TH 378,080 18,904 30,247; 7TH 371,969 18,599 29,758; 8TH 360,817 18,041 28,866.

All petitioners must deliver signed petitions to the secretary of state's office no less than six months before the election. For the 2026 election cycle, the submission deadline for signatures is 5:00 PM on May 3. ... For the 2026 election cycle, the number of valid signatures needed is outlined on page 5 (Table A). ... MINIMUM SIGNATURES REQUIRED 106,384 / 170,215.

Each petition circulator shall be at least eighteen years of age and registered with the secretary of state. Signatures collected by any circulator who has not registered with the secretary of state pursuant to this chapter on or before 5:00 p.m. on the final day for filing petitions with the secretary of state shall not be counted. A petition circulator shall be deemed registered at the time such circulator delivers a signed circulator's affidavit pursuant to section 116.030, with respect to a referendum petition, or section 116.040, with respect to an initiative petition, to the office of the secretary of state. No person shall qualify as a petition circulator who has been convicted of, found guilty of, or pled guilty to an offense involving forgery.

deadline.headline: n/a — no citizen initiative process

rules.value: no functioning citizen initiative
rules.note: NO CURRENTLY AVAILABLE CITIZEN INITIATIVE. Mississippi's statewide citizen-initiative process (Miss. Const. art. 15, Sec. 273, an INDIRECT initiated constitutional amendment process adopted 1992) was struck down by the Mississippi Supreme Court in Butler v. Watson (May 14, 2021). The court held the signature-distributi

compensation.operatingStance: Moot. There is no operative initiative process in Mississippi, so there are no current statutory or regulatory rules governing circulator residency or per-signature pay for initiatives. The former process lived in the state constitution (Section 273) plus Title 23, Chapter 17 of the Mississippi Code; both are now inoperative following Butler v. Watson (2021). Any future restore

voterFile.cost: Free. The full statewide voter file and statewide voter history file are distributed weekly "at no cost" to anyone who signs up. (The alternative one-off public-records-request path yields the same file; the SOS does not publish a separate fee for it on this page.)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Open to anyone who signs up for the Statewide Voter File Distribution List — no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate is stated. The SOS confirms it is "the same type of file provided when a statewide voter file is requested through a public records request," i.e., it is a public record available to the general public. Voters' SSNs, birthdates, and other private info are excluded by Mississippi law and are not released.
voterFile.useRestrictions: No commercial-use prohibition, anti-solicitation clause, political-use-only limit, or penalty is stated in the distribution-program document or the SOS Elections page. The only stated limit is on the SOS's side: it "will not resend or forward any emails of the weekly distribution list," and private fields (SSN, DOB) are statutorily withheld. (Use restrictions in Miss. Code §23-15-165 governing the Statewide Elections Management System could not be retrieved verbatim — legal mirrors were blocked — so the absence of restrictions is based on the primary SOS sources only.)
voterFile.requestProcess: Self-service sign-up to a recurring distribution list, then automated weekly delivery. Enroll via the "Statewide Voter File Distribution List" Smartsheet form (linked from the SOS "Request Voter File" PDF: https://app.smartsheet.com/b/form/5c8b666a679c4c148eb513c8981a4962). Enrollees then "receive an email each week with a Sharefile link" to download the file. No application approval, affidavit, or notarized sworn statement is described for the weekly program; any request outside the weekly distribution must go through the formal public records request process.

verification.riskNote: NO CURRENTLY AVAILABLE CITIZEN INITIATIVE. Mississippi's statewide citizen-initiative process (Miss. Const. art. 15, Sec. 273, an INDIRECT initiated constitutional amendment process adopted 1992) was struck down by the Mississippi Supreme Court in Butler v. Watson (May 14, 2021). The court held the signature-distribution requirement — signatures had to be gathered roughly equally from each of FIVE congressional districts — was unworkable and the entire Section 273 process inv

citations.quote: Mississippi's ballot initiative process has been in limbo since 2021, when the state Supreme Court struck it down in Butler v. Watson. The justices ruled that the constitutional requirement for signatures from five congressional districts was unworkable, since Mississippi has only had four districts since 2001.

Despite the Mississippi Constitution explicitly stating that voters still have a right to offer amendments through an initiative process, citizens have no process to change state laws or the state Constitution.

The committee voted to approve Senate Concurrent Resolution 518, which would require initiative organizers to gather signatures from at least 10% of registered voters in the state, or roughly 170,000 signatures, before it can go on a ballot... As it's currently written, England's resolution would allow a ballot initiative to change or create state law, but not the state Constitution, as the previous ballot initiative system provided. It would also prohibit initiatives related to abortion and the state's public pension system... The last day for the chamber to adopt the measure on the Senate floor is Feb. 12.

It now heads to the full Senate floor, where at least two-thirds of the chamber's members have to approve it before it can advance to a House committee. If it passes the Legislature, the initiative process would have to be ratified by voters in a statewide election.
The bill to restore Mississippi's voters' righ to put issues on the ballot faces a Thursday night deadline for passage and is not likely to be brought back up again in the Senate.
For five years since the state Supreme Court invalidated Mississippi's ballot initiative process, lawmakers have been unable to agree on a plan to reinstate the right to voters.
Republican Rep. Price Wallace was known at the Mississippi Capitol for his big cowboy hat, his knowledge of agriculture issues and his work to restore the ballot initiative process.

According to State law, as interpreted by the Mississippi Supreme Court, for an initiative measure to be placed on the ballot, a minimum of 106,190 certified signatures must be gathered from the congressional district as they exist at the time a petition is presented for approval. No more than twenty percent of signatures of qualified electors may come from a single congressional district.
Not only must an initiative receive a majority of the total votes cast for that particular initiative, it must also receive more than 40% of the total votes cast in that election.
Samantha Buckley, policy director at the nonpartisan Secure Democracy Foundation, told lawmakers the fix could be as simple as changing 'five' to 'four' in the constitution.

10% of the qualified electors in each of two-fifths (40) of the state's 100 legislative representative districts (Montana Const. Art. III, Sec. 4; MCA 13-27-241(1)(a)). Note: 2002 amendments C-37/C-38 attempting a county basis were struck down in Montana PIRG v. Johnson; basis is legislative districts.
Yes — single-subject rule applies (Montana Const. Art. III, Sec. 4; Art. XIV, Sec. 11). A measure may embrace only one subject.

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Per-signature pay is EXPRESSLY BANNED. MCA 13-27-102(2): "A person gathering signatures for a petition may not be paid anything of value based upon the number of signatures gathered." Paid signature gatherers ARE otherwise permitted (paid by time/salary), but as of 2025 (Ch. 239, L. 2025) they must verbally disclose their first name, the state in which they legally reside, and

voterFile.cost: $1,000.00 flat for a single copy of the complete statewide voter file (or any single extract such as the Absentee Ballot Report or Petition Signers Report); $5,000.00 for a one-year (365-day) subscription giving unlimited downloads of all extracts/reports. Paid by Visa/MasterCard (credit or debit) online.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any individual, for noncommercial use. By statute (13-2-122 MCA) the Secretary of State "shall furnish to any individual, for noncommercial use" extracts and reports from the statewide voter registration system — no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate. A campaign or committee qualifies as long as the use is noncommercial.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Noncommercial use only. Statute 13-2-122(1) limits furnishing to "noncommercial use"; the SOS voter file service states records "are for non-commercial use only." The SOS notes that if a requester needs a legal definition of noncommercial use, they should consult an attorney. Per 13-2-122(2), residential addresses of electors whose address is protected under 13-2-115(6)/(7) are withheld (name only listed). No solicitation/penalty language quoted on the SOS page.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online purchase through the SOS centralized voter file service at app.mt.gov/voterfile. The requester customizes a file by criteria (county, district, etc.), pays by credit/debit card, and is emailed a download link once the file is generated. A one-time purchase requires no account beyond payment; an annual subscription requires signing up for an Okta (okta.mt.gov) account. No notarized affidavit or sworn statement of permitted use is described.

verification.riskNote: Current-cycle (2026) valid-signature requirements computed off the 2024 gubernatorial general-election vote (statewide ~602,400 votes for governor): ICA = 10% = 60,241; ISS / veto referendum = 5% = 30,121 (per Ballotpedia 2026 deadlines page). Numbers recompute each cycle on the most recent gubernatorial vote. Distribution is the binding constraint in practice: ICA needs 10% of each of 40 districts; ISS/VR needs 5% of each of 34 districts. GATE = CONSERVATIVE-HOLD for two rea

citations.quote: If 10% of the voters in each of two-fifths of the legislative representative districts (totaling 40 legislative representative districts) sign this petition and the total number of voters signing the petition is _____, this constitutional amendment will appear on the next general election ballot. If a majority of voters vote for this amendment at that election, it will become part of the constitution.

If 5% of the voters in each of one-third of the legislative representative districts (totaling 34 legislative representative districts) sign this petition and the total number of voters signing this petition is _____, this initiative will appear on the next general election ballot. If a majority of voters vote for this initiative at that election, it will become law.

If 5% of the voters in each of 34 legislative representative districts sign this petition and the total number of voters signing the petition is ____, Senate (House) Bill Number ____ will appear on the next general election ballot. If a majority of voters vote for this referendum at that election it will become law.

(1) A petition may be signed only by a qualified elector of the state of Montana. (2) A person gathering signatures for a petition may not be paid anything of value based upon the number of signatures gathered.

Unless a specific time for filing is provided in the constitution, all petitions filed with the secretary of state, certified as provided by law, must be received before 5 p.m. of the third Friday of the fourth month prior to the election at which they are to be voted upon by the people.

A paid signature gatherer, as defined in 13-27-112, shall verbally disclose their first name, the state in which they legally reside, and their status as a paid signature gatherer to anyone they approach about signing a petition. The paid signature gatherer shall also wear a badge stating this information, including their full first name and last initial.
A statewide initiative filed under the provisions of this chapter may not be filed if it is substantially the same as a measure defeated by the voters in an election within the preceding 4 years.

Montana | CICA | 06/21/2026 | Signatures must be verified on or before the third Friday of June | 60,241   Montana | CISS | 06/21/2026 | Signatures must be verified on or before the third Friday of June | 30,121

To place an initiated constitutional amendment on the ballot, proponents must collect valid signatures signatures equal to 10 percent of votes cast for governor in the last gubernatorial general election. For an initiated state statute or veto referendum, signatures equal to 5 percent of votes cast for governor in the most recent gubernatorial election are required.

citations.field: distribution (qualified-electors basis = votes for governor in district) — corroboration
citations.quote: For a constitutional amendment, proponents must collect signatures equal to 10 percent of the qualified electors in each of two-fifths (40) of the state's 100 legislative districts. For an initiated statute or a veto referendum, signatures must be equal to 5 percent of the qualified electors in each of one-third (34) of the state's legislative districts. The number of qualified electors in any district, for the purpose of the distribution requirement, is equal to the number of votes cast for governor in that district in the last gubernatorial election.

Montana does not require signature gatherers to be residents of the state. In Pierce et al. v. Jacobsen et al. (2022), a three-judge panel of the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that Montana cannot restrict circulators from out-of-state.

The petition must contain signatures equal to 5 percent of votes cast for governor from at least one-third of legislative districts to qualify the referendum for the ballot. If the petition contains signatures equal to 15 percent of votes cast for governor from a majority of legislative districts, it suspends the targeted law until the election.

By statute (13-2-122 MCA) the Secretary of State "shall furnish to any individual, for noncommercial use" extracts and reports from the statewide voter registration system — no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate. A campaign or committee qualifies as long as the use is noncommercial.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Noncommercial use only. Statute 13-2-122(1) limits furnishing to "noncommercial use"; the SOS voter file service states records "are for non-commercial use only." The SOS notes that if a requester needs a legal definition of noncommercial use, they should consult an attorney. Per 13-2-122(2), residential addresses of electors whose address is protected under 13-2-115(6)/(7) are withheld (name only listed). No solicitation/penalty language quoted on the SOS page.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online purchase through the SOS centralized voter file service at app.mt.gov/voterfile. The requester customizes a file by criteria (county, district, etc.), pays by credit/debit card, and is emailed a download link once the file is generated. A one-time purchase requires no account beyond payment; an annual subscription requires signing up for an Okta (okta.mt.gov) account. No notarized affidavit or sworn statement of permitted use is described.

If a majority of voters vote for this amendment at that election, it will become part of the constitution.

If 5% of the voters in each of one-third of the legislative representative districts (totaling 34 legislative representative districts) sign this petition and the total number of voters signing this petition is _____, this initiative will appear on the next general election ballot. (1) A petition may be signed only by a qualified elector of the state of Montana. (2) A person gathering signatures for a petition may not be paid anything of value based upon the number of signatures gathered.

Unless a specific time for filing is provided in the constitution, all petitions filed with the secretary of state, certified as provided by law, must be received before 5 p.m. of the third Friday of the fourth month prior to the election at which they are to be voted upon by the people.

A paid signature gatherer, as defined in 13-27-112, shall verbally disclose their first name, the state in which they legally reside, and their status as a paid signature gatherer to anyone they approach about signing a petition. The paid signature gatherer shall also wear a badge stating this information, including their full first name and last initial.

citations.quote: For a constitutional amendment, proponents must collect signatures equal to 10 percent of the qualified electors in each of two-fifths (40) of the state's 100 legislative districts. For an initiated statute or a veto referendum, signatures must be equal to 5 percent of the qualified electors in each of one-third (34) of the state's legislative districts. The number of qualified electors in any district, for the purpose of the distribution requirement, is equal to the number of votes cast for governor in that district in the last gubernatorial election.

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Per-signature pay for circulators is EXPLICITLY BANNED for initiative, referendum, and recall petitions. voterFile.cost: N/A — no statewide voter registration file exists for sale or download. North Dakota has no voter registration. The state's internal "central voter file" (NDVOICES) is restricted to election officials via a secure login; it is not offered as a purchasable/downloadable public product, so no public price list exists. If pursued at all, treat as "varies / not publicly available."
voterFile.whoCanObtain: No general public voter-file product. The central voter record system (NDVOICES — North Dakota Voter Information & Central Election Systems) is accessible only to election officials (Secretary of State Elections Division and the 53 county auditors) through a secure login area. There is no candidate/committee voter-list purchase program comparable to other states, because there is no registration list.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Not applicable as a sold dataset. The central voter record is an internal election-administration system (it even flags non-citizens for officials). For petition use, the binding legal constraint is that ballot-measure/nominating petition signers must be North Dakota qualified electors (U.S. citizen, 18+, ND resident 30 days); SOS/county auditors review signatures against signer-supplied name + residential address + date. A campaign cannot obtain a state registration roster to pre-match signers, because none is published.
voterFile.requestProcess: No standard request product. There is no online download, application form, or affidavit process to buy a statewide voter file, because ND does not register voters. Practical signature verification is done by the official filing officer (Secretary of State for statewide measures; county auditors for local). Any inquiry about voter-record data goes to the SOS Elections Division (701-328-4146), but the central file is not made available to campaigns as a list.

CURRENT CYCLE (2026 election): constitutional amendment = 31,164 valid signatures (4% of 2020 census 779,094); initiated statute = 15,582 (2%); veto referendum = 15,582 (2%). These numbers are FIXED for the entire 2021-2030 decade because ND ties the requirement to the decennial census, not to votes cast — they will not change until the 2030 census. Base year = 2020 (population 779,094). Verified independently

citations.quote: While the legislative power of this state shall be vested in a legislative assembly consisting of a senate and a house of representatives, the people reserve the power to propose and enact laws by the initiative, including the call for a constitutional convention; to approve or reject legislative Acts, or parts thereof, by the referendum; to propose and adopt constitutional amendments by the initiative; and to recall certain elected officials. This article is self-executing and all of its provisions are mandatory. Laws may be enacted to facilitate and safeguard, but not to hamper, restrict, or impair these powers.

Text of Section 4: The petition may be submitted to the secretary of state if signed by electors equal in number to two percent of the resident population of the state at the last federal decennial census.

Text of Section 9: A constitutional amendment may be proposed by initiative petition. If signed by electors equal in number to four percent of the resident population of the state at the last federal decennial census, the petition may be submitted to the secretary of state. All other provisions relating to initiative measures apply hereto.

Constitutional Measure - changes the constitution of the State of North Dakota which can only be accomplished by a vote of the people. Petitions require 4% of the population (31,164 signatures)Initiated Measure - changes North Dakota Century Code which can be accomplished by a vote of the people or by the state legislature. Petitions require 2% of the population (15,582 signatures)

A sponsoring committee looking to circulate a statewide ballot measure petition must first have the form of the petition approved by the Secretary of State. They then have 1 year to circulate the petition and gather signatures. However, the petition must be filed 120 days before the Primary or General Elections in order to be placed on the respective ballot for that election.

An initiative petition shall be submitted not less than one twenty hundred days before the statewide election at which the measure is to be voted upon. A referendum petition may be submitted only within ninety days after the filing of the measure with the secretary of state.

A referendum petition may be submitted only within ninety days after the filing of the measure with the secretary of state. The submission of a petition shall suspend the operation of any measure enacted by the legislative assembly except emergency measures and appropriation measures for the support and maintenance of state departments and institutions.

Text of Section 3: The petition shall be circulated only by electors. They shall swear thereon that the electors who have signed the petition did so in their presence. Each elector signing a petition shall also write in the date of signing and his post-office address.

Pay or offer to pay any individual, measure committee, or other organization, or receive payment or agree to receive payment, on a basis related to the number of signatures obtained for circulating an initiative, referendum, or recall petition. This subsection does not prohibit the payment of salary and expenses for circulation of the petition on a basis not related to the number of signatures obtained, as long as the circulators file the intent to remunerate before submitting the petitions ... Any signature obtained in violation of this subdivision is void and may not be counted.
North Dakota does not have a distribution requirement for petition signatures.
initiated measures and amendments are not governed by subject restrictions in North Dakota. In addition, they are not required to specify a funding source for mandated expenditures.

A prohibition against the circulation of a petition to initiate a constitutional amendment that would directly appropriate public funds or require lawmakers to directly appropriate funds for a specific purpose was approved by the North Dakota legislature in 2013.
The Peace Garden State grew 15.8% from 2010 to 2020, to a population of 779,094.

Since North Dakota does not have a voter registration system, the number of signatures required is tied to the population reported by the last decennial census. As such, any US Citizen who is at least 18 years old and who has resided in the state for at least thirty days may sign an initiative petition. For statutes and veto referendums, the number of signatures required is 2 percent of the population. For constitutional amendments, it is 4 percent of the population.

In North Dakota, no law could be found that prohibited a circulator from signing his/her own petition... circulators are required to be at least 18 years of age or older and to be an elector in the state of North Dakota. Moreover, paid circulators must be registered with the secretary of state. Volunteers do not have to register.

Any inquiry about voter-record data goes to the SOS Elections Division (701-328-4146), but the central file is not made available to campaigns as a list.

verification.riskNote: All figures are primary-source-grounded and settled, so AUTO-LIVE. CURRENT CYCLE (2026 election): constitutional amendment = 31,164 valid signatures (4% of 2020 census 779,094); initiated statute = 15,582 (2%); veto referendum = 15,582 (2%). These numbers are FIXED for the entire 2021-2030 decade because ND ties the requirement to the decennial census, not to votes cast — they will not change until the 2030 census. Base year = 2020 (population 779,094). Verified independently

citations.quote: While the legislative power of this state shall be vested in a legislative assembly consisting of a senate and a house of representatives, the people reserve the power to propose and enact laws by the initiative, including the call for a constitutional convention; to approve or reject legislative Acts, or parts thereof, by the referendum; to propose and adopt constitutional amendments by the initiative; and to recall certain elected officials. This article is self-executing and all of its provisions are mandatory. Laws may be enacted to facilitate and safeguard, but not to hamper, restrict, or impair these powers.

Text of Section 4: The petition may be submitted to the secretary of state if signed by electors equal in number to two percent of the resident population of the state at the last federal decennial census.

Text of Section 9: A constitutional amendment may be proposed by initiative petition. If signed by electors equal in number to four percent of the resident population of the state at the last federal decennial census, the petition may be submitted to the secretary of state. All other provisions relating to initiative measures apply hereto.

Constitutional Measure - changes the constitution of the State of North Dakota which can only be accomplished by a vote of the people. Petitions require 4% of the population (31,164 signatures)Initiated Measure - changes North Dakota Century Code which can be accomplished by a vote of the people or by the state legislature. Petitions require 2% of the population (15,582 signatures)

A sponsoring committee looking to circulate a statewide ballot measure petition must first have the form of the petition approved by the Secretary of State. They then have 1 year to circulate the petition and gather signatures. However, the petition must be filed 120 days before the Primary or General Elections in order to be placed on the respective ballot for that election.

An initiative petition shall be submitted not less than one twenty hundred days before the statewide election at which the measure is to be voted upon. A referendum petition may be submitted only within ninety days after the filing of the measure with the secretary of state.

A referendum petition may be submitted only within ninety days after the filing of the measure with the secretary of state. The submission of a petition shall suspend the operation of any measure enacted by the legislative assembly except emergency measures and appropriation measures for the support and maintenance of state departments and institutions.

Text of Section 3: The petition shall be circulated only by electors. They shall swear thereon that the electors who have signed the petition did so in their presence. Each elector signing a petition shall also write in the date of signing and his post-office address.

Pay or offer to pay any individual, measure committee, or other organization, or receive payment or agree to receive payment, on a basis related to the number of signatures obtained for circulating an initiative, referendum, or recall petition. This subsection does not prohibit the payment of salary and expenses for circulation of the petition on a basis not related to the number of signatures obtained, as long as the circulators file the intent to remunerate before submitting the petitions ... Any signature obtained in violation of this subdivision is void and may not be counted.
North Dakota does not have a distribution requirement for petition signatures.
initiated measures and amendments are not governed by subject restrictions in North Dakota. In addition, they are not required to specify a funding source for mandated expenditures.

A prohibition against the circulation of a petition to initiate a constitutional amendment that would directly appropriate public funds or require lawmakers to directly appropriate funds for a specific purpose was approved by the North Dakota legislature in 2013.
The Peace Garden State grew 15.8% from 2010 to 2020, to a population of 779,094.

Since North Dakota does not have a voter registration system, the number of signatures required is tied to the population reported by the last decennial census. As such, any US Citizen who is at least 18 years old and who has resided in the state for at least thirty days may sign an initiative petition. For statutes and veto referendums, the number of signatures required is 2 percent of the population. For constitutional amendments, it is 4 percent of the population.

In North Dakota, no law could be found that prohibited a circulator from signing his/her own petition... circulators are required to be at least 18 years of age or older and to be an elector in the state of North Dakota. Moreover, paid circulators must be registered with the secretary of state. Volunteers do not have to register.

signatures.headline: 10% of the registered voters of the state (Neb. Const. art. III, sec. 2: "if the petition be for the amendment of the Constitution, the petition therefore shall be signed by ten percent of such registered voters"). Base = registered voters as of the signature-filing deadline; the old "votes cast for Governor" basis in sec. 4 was superseded by the registered-voter language (Duggan v. Beermann, 245 Neb. 907 (1994)).

deadline.headline: "filed with the Secretary of State who shall submit the measure thus proposed to the electors of the state at the first general election held not less than four months after such petition shall have been filed" (Neb. Const. art. III, sec. 2). For the Nov. 3, 2026 general election the signature-filing deadline is on or before July 3, 2026 (four months prior).

Yes — "Initiative measures shall contain only one subject." (Neb. Const. art. III, sec. 2.) Nebraska Supreme Court applies a "natural and necessary connection" test (State ex rel. Wagner v. Evnen, 2020).

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature pay for INITIATIVE/REFERENDUM circulators is ALLOWED in Nebraska. The prior statutory ban on pay-per-signature was removed by LB 367 (2015); Nebraska is NOT one of the ~10 states banning per-sig pay for initiatives. Nebraska law requires that each circulator's PAID or VOLUNTEER status be disclosed on the petition form in large red lettering (Neb. Rev. Stat. ch. 32

voterFile.cost: $500 flat for the statewide Nebraska Voter Registration file (annual affidavit + fee); once paid and the affidavit is approved, you may request updated files for one full year. Payable by cash or check/money order to the Secretary of State. (A new file is generated the first day of each month.)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any requester who completes and signs the affidavit (Public Service Request Form) attesting to a permitted use. Not open-access/instant download to the general public — the file is gated behind a sworn permitted-use affidavit and the $500 fee, and use is limited to elections, political activities, voter registration, law enforcement, and jury selection.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Permitted uses only: elections, political activities, voter registration, law enforcement, jury selection. The file "shall not be posted, displayed, or used for commercial purposes or made accessible on the Internet." Recipient must disclose any security breach of the stored data to the Secretary of State without delay. Verifying petition signatures (matching signers to registered voters) is a permitted election/political-activity use.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application/affidavit + approval + payment. Complete the "Public Service Request Form for the Nebraska Central Voter Registration System" (voter-file-affidavit.pdf), submit it to the Elections Division with the $500 fee; upon payment and approval of the affidavit you may request the file (refreshed monthly) for one year. Submit to: Nebraska Secretary of State Elections Division, sos.elect@nebraska.gov / 402-471-2555 / P.O. Box 94608, Lincoln, NE 68509-4608.

No more than one act or portion of an act of the Legislature shall be the subject of each referendum petition... When the referendum is invoked as to any act or part of act, other than emergency acts or those for the immediate preservation of the public peace, health, or safety, by petition signed by not less than ten percent of the registered voters of the state distributed as aforesaid, it shall suspend the taking effect of such act or part of act until the same has been approved by the electors of the state.

The part of Section 2 which refers to registered voters repeals the reference in Section 4 below which refers to those voting in the preceding gubernatorial election. As per Section 2 above, the number of signatures required for placement of an initiative petition on the ballot by the Nebraska Constitution is equal to 10 percent of the number of registered voters on the date the signatures are to be turned in. This was litigated in Duggan v. Beermann, 245 Neb. 907, 515 N.W.2d 788 (1994).

signatures.headline: Nevada Constitution Art. 19, Sec. 2(2): "An initiative petition ... shall be proposed by a number of registered voters equal to 10 percent or more of the number of voters who voted at the last preceding general election ... but the total number of registered voters signing the initiative petition shall be equal to 10 percent or more of the voters who voted in the entire State at the last preceding general election." Base = total votes cast at the 2024 general election.

deadline.headline: Constitution Art. 19, Sec. 2(4): petition "shall be filed with the Secretary of State not less than 90 days before any regular general election." Per Ballotpedia (2026 cycle): "06/24/2026 ... Signatures must be filed by 15 days after the primary election" (petitions due with county officials 15 days after the 2026 primary, who forward to SoS).

rules.value: Nevada Constitution Art. 19, Sec. 2(2): "An initiative petition ... shall be proposed by a number of registered voters equal to 10 percent or more of the number of voters who voted at the last preceding general election ... but the total number of registered voters signing the initiative petition shall be equal to 10 percent or more of the voters who voted in the entire State at the last preceding general election." Base = total votes cast at the 2024 general election.
rules.note: direct/indirect: direct (proceeds directly to voters; but must be approved at TWO consecutive general elections per Art. 19, Sec. 2(4))

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature pay for initiative/referendum circulators is PERMITTED in Nevada. Ballotpedia's Pay-per-signature page lists Nevada in the \"Permitted\" column, citing NRS Chapter 295; Nevada is NOT among the ~10 states that ban per-signature compensation (AZ, AR, FL, MT, ND, OK [added 2025], OR, SD, UT, WY). Nevada \"law does not require a circulator's paid/volunteer status to b

voterFile.cost: Statutory rate = 1 cent ($0.01) per name (NRS 293.440(1)). For the full statewide file (~2.07M active voters) this works out to roughly $20,000; Ballotpedia lists the statewide-list price as "$20,000." A campaign normally requests a filtered subset (e.g., one county/district) and pays $0.01 per record, so verifying petition signers in a targeted geography is typically well under $100. Major/minor political-party central/executive committees can obtain the list without charge.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Effectively open / general public ("NevVoter is accessible to the public"), but the Secretary of State "retains full authority in granting, denying or revoking access at any time." A campaign or committee qualifies. The SOS verifies the applicant's qualification when the request form is filed. Major-party state/county central committees and minor-party executive committees may obtain copies without charge under NRS 293.440(1).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes — political/election-use-only. Per NRS 293.440(6)(b) (and as restated on the SOS page): "any person, candidate, party or organization who receives information requested on this form is prohibited from using such information for purposes not related to an election and from selling such information for any reason." The statute also bars selling the list "for compensation or other valuable consideration."
voterFile.requestProcess: Two-step: (1) create a web account in the Nevada SOS Online Services portal; (2) download, complete, sign, and file the "Official Request for List of Registered Voters Form" with the SOS (by email, fax, mail, or in person). The form attests to permitted election-related use. Access is granted/denied after verification, typically the next business day up to 7 business days, and the applicant is notified by email. (Not an instant anonymous download — application form + approval.)

citations.quote: Whenever a number of registered voters of this state equal to 10 percent or more of the number of voters who voted at the last preceding general election shall express their wish by filing with the secretary of state, not less than 120 days before the next general election, a petition in the form provided for in Section 3 of this Article that any statute or resolution or any part thereof enacted by the legislature be submitted to a vote of the people

An initiative petition shall be in the form required by Section 3 of this Article and shall be proposed by a number of registered voters equal to 10 percent or more of the number of voters who voted at the last preceding general election in not less than 75 percent of the counties in the State, but the total number of registered voters signing the initiative petition shall be equal to 10 percent or more of the voters who voted in the entire State at the last preceding general election.

If the initiative petition proposes a statute or an amendment to a statute, the person who intends to circulate it shall file a copy with the Secretary of State before beginning circulation and not earlier than January 1 of the year preceding the year in which a regular session of the Legislature is held. After its circulation, it shall be filed with the Secretary of State not less than 30 days prior to any regular session of the Legislature.

If the initiative petition proposes an amendment to the Constitution, the person who intends to circulate it shall file a copy with the Secretary of State before beginning circulation and not earlier than September 1 of the year before the year in which the election is to be held. After its circulation it shall be filed with the Secretary of State not less than 90 days before any regular general election ... If a majority of such voters votes approval of such amendment, the Secretary of State shall publish and resubmit the question of approval or disapproval to a vote of the voters at the next succeeding general election in the same manner as such question was originally submitted.

Ballotpedia's Pay-per-signature page lists Nevada in the \"Permitted\" column, citing NRS Chapter 295; Nevada is NOT among the ~10 states that ban per-signature compensation (AZ, AR, FL, MT, ND, OK [added 2025], OR, SD, UT, WY). voterFile.cost: Statutory rate = 1 cent ($0.01) per name (NRS 293.440(1)). For the full statewide file (~2.07M active voters) this works out to roughly $20,000; Ballotpedia lists the statewide-list price as "$20,000." A campaign normally requests a filtered subset (e.g., one county/district) and pays $0.01 per record, so verifying petition signers in a targeted geography is typically well under $100. Major/minor political-party central/executive committees can obtain the list without charge.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Effectively open / general public ("NevVoter is accessible to the public"), but the Secretary of State "retains full authority in granting, denying or revoking access at any time." A campaign or committee qualifies. The SOS verifies the applicant's qualification when the request form is filed. Major-party state/county central committees and minor-party executive committees may obtain copies without charge under NRS 293.440(1).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes — political/election-use-only. Per NRS 293.440(6)(b) (and as restated on the SOS page): "any person, candidate, party or organization who receives information requested on this form is prohibited from using such information for purposes not related to an election and from selling such information for any reason." The statute also bars selling the list "for compensation or other valuable consideration."
voterFile.requestProcess: Two-step: (1) create a web account in the Nevada SOS Online Services portal; (2) download, complete, sign, and file the "Official Request for List of Registered Voters Form" with the SOS (by email, fax, mail, or in person). The form attests to permitted election-related use. Access is granted/denied after verification, typically the next business day up to 7 business days, and the applicant is notified by email. (Not an instant anonymous download — application form + approval.)

voterFile.cost: Free. The entire statewide voter registration file is downloadable at no charge from the Ohio Secretary of State's Voter Files Download Page (no fee, no account, no form).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — anyone. The statewide voter file is treated as a public record and posted online for open download with no eligibility gate (no candidate/committee/registered-user restriction).
voterFile.useRestrictions: No use-restriction gate is imposed at the point of download (no affidavit, click-through use agreement, or commercial-use prohibition on the SoS download page itself). General Ohio public-records/voter-registration law applies; the SoS states the files are published "in accordance with Ohio law." No purpose attestation is required to obtain the file, which is ideal for petition-signature matching.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online download. Go to the SoS Voter Files Download Page, select "Statewide Voter Files," and click each of the four files to download directly. No request, form, approval, or payment.

These match Ballotpedia's published 2026 figures (413,487 / 124,046 / 248,093) — the 1-signature difference on the ICA is rounding/truncation of the 10% computation (413,487

citations.quote: The first aforestated power reserved by the people is designated the initiative, and the signatures of ten per centum of the electors shall be required upon a petition to propose an amendment to the constitution.

the secretary of state shall submit for the approval or rejection of the electors, the proposed amendment, in the manner hereinafter provided, at the next succeeding regular or general election in any year occurring subsequent to one hundred twenty-five days after the filing of such petition.

When at any time, not less than ten days prior to the commencement of any session of the General Assembly, there shall have been filed with the secretary of state a petition signed by three per centum of the electors and verified as herein provided, proposing a law ... it shall be submitted by the secretary of state to the electors for their approval or rejection, if such submission shall be demanded by supplementary petition verified as herein provided and signed by not less than three per centum of the electors in addition to those signing the original petition, which supplementary petition must be signed and filed with the secretary of state within ninety days after the proposed law shall have been rejected by the General Assembly or after the expiration of such term of four months, if no action has been taken thereon

The second aforestated power reserved by the people is designated the referendum, and the signatures of six per centum of the electors shall be required upon a petition to order the submission to the electors of the state for their approval or rejection, of any law, section of any law or any item in any law appropriating money passed by the General Assembly.

Ohio law instead requires DISCLOSURE: under ORC 3501.38(E) (and the mandatory circulator statement under ORC 3501.38 / 3519.05) the circulator must disclose whether they were paid or were a volunteer, completed in the cir

voterFile.cost: Free. The entire statewide voter registration file is downloadable at no charge from the Ohio Secretary of State's Voter Files Download Page (no fee, no account, no form).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — anyone. The statewide voter file is treated as a public record and posted online for open download with no eligibility gate (no candidate/committee/registered-user restriction).
voterFile.useRestrictions: No use-restriction gate is imposed at the point of download (no affidavit, click-through use agreement, or commercial-use prohibition on the SoS download page itself). General Ohio public-records/voter-registration law applies; the SoS states the files are published "in accordance with Ohio law." No purpose attestation is required to obtain the file, which is ideal for petition-signature matching.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online download. Go to the SoS Voter Files Download Page, select "Statewide Voter Files," and click each of the four files to download directly. No request, form, approval, or payment.

citations.quote: The first aforestated power reserved by the people is designated the initiative, and the signatures of ten per centum of the electors shall be required upon a petition to propose an amendment to the constitution.

the secretary of state shall submit for the approval or rejection of the electors, the proposed amendment, in the manner hereinafter provided, at the next succeeding regular or general election in any year occurring subsequent to one hundred twenty-five days after the filing of such petition.

When at any time, not less than ten days prior to the commencement of any session of the General Assembly, there shall have been filed with the secretary of state a petition signed by three per centum of the electors and verified as herein provided, proposing a law ... it shall be submitted by the secretary of state to the electors for their approval or rejection, if such submission shall be demanded by supplementary petition verified as herein provided and signed by not less than three per centum of the electors in addition to those signing the original petition, which supplementary petition must be signed and filed with the secretary of state within ninety days after the proposed law shall have been rejected by the General Assembly or after the expiration of such term of four months, if no action has been taken thereon

The second aforestated power reserved by the people is designated the referendum, and the signatures of six per centum of the electors shall be required upon a petition to order the submission to the electors of the state for their approval or rejection, of any law, section of any law or any item in any law appropriating money passed by the General Assembly.

Per Okla. Const. Art. V, Sec. 3 and 34 O.S. Sec. 8 as amended

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Per-signature pay for petition circulators is EXPRESSLY BANNED for both initiatives and referendums. 34 O.S. Sec. 8.G.1 (as amended by SB 1027, 2025): 'No compensation shall be based on number of signatures collected, number of signature sheets submitted, or any other similar incentives.' The same provision adds an out-of-state funding/compensation ban: 'No person or entity who

voterFile.cost: Free (no charge). "The Secretary of the State Election Board shall ensure that an electronic copy of the statewide voter registration list is made available, at no cost" via the OK Election Data Warehouse (EDW). No per-record or admin fee.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated (effective Nov. 1, 2025, per 26 O.S. § 7-103.2). Full access to the voter registration list is limited to: bona fide Oklahoma residents who are U.S. citizens; official representatives of recognized Oklahoma political parties; candidates for Oklahoma office and their official representatives; or other persons authorized by state law. A political campaign/committee qualifies as a candidate's representative. (Non-qualifying requesters may get only "basic" EDW access, which excludes voter registration lists.)
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes. Requestor must acknowledge it is unlawful to use the voter registration list for commercial marketing or advertising purposes (this restriction does not apply to campaigns, political parties, government officials, or public opinion pollsters), and that it is unlawful to knowingly make the Oklahoma voter list available to a foreign national, foreign commercial interest, or foreign government entity. The eligibility attestation is made under penalty of perjury. Driver license and SSN are never released; Restricted Records / Address Confidentiality Program participants are excluded.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form + approval. Submit the "Request to Access Voter Registration List" form to info@elections.ok.gov (or by fax 405-521-6457 / mail). The form requires attestation "under penalty of perjury" that the requestor meets the legal requirements, plus a signed acknowledgment of the use restrictions. After processing, an automated email provides a link, username, and password to download the statewide CSV from the EDW. Access is valid for one year and renewable.
