All three match Ballotpedia's 2026 figures and recompute from the verbatim Art. V, Sec. 2 formula. The signature base will reset after the November 2026 gubernatorial general election for any petition wh

citations.quote: The first power reserved by the people is the initiative, and eight per centum of the legal voters shall have the right to propose any legislative measure, and fifteen per centum of the legal voters shall have the right to propose amendments to the Constitution by petition, and every such petition shall include the full text of the measure so proposed. The second power is the referendum, and it may be ordered (except as to laws necessary for the immediate preservation of the public peace, health, or safety), either by petition signed by five per centum of the legal voters or by the Legislature as other bills are enacted. The ratio and per centum of legal voters hereinbefore stated shall be based upon the total number votes cast at the last general election for the Office of Governor.

Referendum petitions shall be filed with the Secretary of State not more than ninety (90) days after the final adjournment of the session of the Legislature which passed the bill on which the referendum is demanded. ... Petitions and orders for the initiative and for the referendum shall be filed with the Secretary of State and addressed to the Governor of the state, who shall submit the same to the people.

Text of Section 6:  Subsequent Proposal of Rejected Measure   Any measure rejected by the people, through the powers of the initiative and referendum, cannot be again proposed by the initiative within three years thereafter by less than twenty-five per centum of the legal voters.

| **2026** | 172,993 | 92,263 | 57,664 | ... initiated constitutional amendment (ICA): 15% of the votes cast for governor ... initiated state statute (ISS): 8% of the votes cast for governor ... veto referendum (VR): 5% of the votes cast for governor
| 2022 | 1,153,284 | ... In Oklahoma, the number of signatures needed to place a measure on the ballot is based on the total number of votes cast for the governor in the preceding election.

H. 1. The total number of signatures collected to meet the requirements for an initiative petition or referendum petition, amending the Oklahoma Statutes, from a single county shall not exceed eleven and five-tenths percent (11.5%) of the number of votes cast in that county during the most recent statewide general election for Governor. ... 2. The total number of signatures collected ... amending the Oklahoma Constitution, from a single county shall not exceed twenty and eight-tenths percent (20.8%) of the number of votes cast in that county during the most recent statewide general election for Governor.

Any person who circulates the petition to collect signatures shall be a registered voter in this state and shall display a conspicuous notice in any location where the person is collecting signatures whether the person is being paid to circulate the petition and if so, by what person or entity. ... I ... say: That I am at least eighteen (18) years old, a registered voter of this state, and that all signatures on the signature sheet were signed in my presence

G. 1. Any person who circulates the petition to collect signatures shall disclose to the Secretary of State any employer or entity that is compensating the person for the circulation of the petition. No compensation shall be based on number of signatures collected, number of signature sheets submitted, or any other similar incentives. No person or entity who does not reside or do business in this state shall contribute to or compensate a person for circulation of a petition.

Pay-per-signature ... Oklahoma prohibits compensation based on the number of signatures collected, the number of petition sheets submitted, "or any other similar incentives." ... Out-of-state circulators ... Oklahoma requires that signature gatherers be registered voters of the state. In 2025, the legislature enacted this requirement.

Oklahoma has a single-subject rule for both initiated statutes and initiated amendments. However, the language of each requirement could be construed as applying only to legislative acts and legislatively referred constitutional amendments. Court cases concering proposed citizen initiatives, however, clarified that the requirement also applied to citizen-initiated measures.

An Act relating to initiative and referendum; amending 34 O.S. 2021, Sections 3, 6, 8 ... which relate to signatures for petitions and ballot title ... adding qualification for persons circulating petition for signatures ... establishing requirements for certain contributions or compensation ... declaring an emergency. ... It being immediately necessary for the preservation of the public peace, health or safety, an emergency is hereby declared to exist, by reason whereof this act shall take effect and be in full force from and after its passage and approval.

In Oklahoma, proponents may circulate a petition for a period of 90 days. For initiatives, the 90-day period for circulation begins on a day set by the secretary of state between 15 and 30 days after all challenges to the initiative petition are finalized. For referendums, signatures are due 90 days after the adjournment of the legislative session in which the targeted law was passed.

Per-county cap (SB 1027, 2025): signatures from a single county may not exceed eleven and five-tenths percent (11.5%) of the number of votes cast in that county during the most recent statewide general election for Governor.
Veto referendum is filed against an existing act 'against one or more items, sections, or parts of any act' (Art. V, Sec. 4); no separate single-subject petition rule beyond the act being referred.

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Per-signature pay for petition circulators is EXPRESSLY BANNED for both initiatives and referendums. 34 O.S. Sec. 8.G.1 (as amended by SB 1027, 2025): 'No compensation shall be based on number of signatures collected, number of signature sheets submitted, or any other similar incentives.' The same provision adds an out-of-state funding/compensation ban: 'No person or entity who

voterFile.cost: Free (no charge). "The Secretary of the State Election Board shall ensure that an electronic copy of the statewide voter registration list is made available, at no cost" via the OK Election Data Warehouse (EDW). No per-record or admin fee.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated (effective Nov. 1, 2025, per 26 O.S. § 7-103.2). Full access to the voter registration list is limited to: bona fide Oklahoma residents who are U.S. citizens; official representatives of recognized Oklahoma political parties; candidates for Oklahoma office and their official representatives; or other persons authorized by state law. A political campaign/committee qualifies as a candidate's representative. (Non-qualifying requesters may get only "basic" EDW access, which excludes voter registration lists.)
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes. Requestor must acknowledge it is unlawful to use the voter registration list for commercial marketing or advertising purposes (this restriction does not apply to campaigns, political parties, government officials, or public opinion pollsters), and that it is unlawful to knowingly make the Oklahoma voter list available to a foreign national, foreign commercial interest, or foreign government entity. The eligibility attestation is made under penalty of perjury. Driver license and SSN are never released; Restricted Records / Address Confidentiality Program participants are excluded.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form + approval. Submit the "Request to Access Voter Registration List" form to info@elections.ok.gov (or by fax 405-521-6457 / mail). The form requires attestation "under penalty of perjury" that the requestor meets the legal requirements, plus a signed acknowledgment of the use restrictions. After processing, an automated email provides a link, username, and password to download the statewide CSV from the EDW. Access is valid for one year and renewable.

citations.quote: The first power reserved by the people is the initiative, and eight per centum of the legal voters shall have the right to propose any legislative measure, and fifteen per centum of the legal voters shall have the right to propose amendments to the Constitution by petition, and every such petition shall include the full text of the measure so proposed. The second power is the referendum, and it may be ordered (except as to laws necessary for the immediate preservation of the public peace, health, or safety), either by petition signed by five per centum of the legal voters or by the Legislature as other bills are enacted. The ratio and per centum of legal voters hereinbefore stated shall be based upon the total number votes cast at the last general election for the Office of Governor.

Referendum petitions shall be filed with the Secretary of State not more than ninety (90) days after the final adjournment of the session of the Legislature which passed the bill on which the referendum is demanded. ... Petitions and orders for the initiative and for the referendum shall be filed with the Secretary of State and addressed to the Governor of the state, who shall submit the same to the people.

Text of Section 6:  Subsequent Proposal of Rejected Measure   Any measure rejected by the people, through the powers of the initiative and referendum, cannot be again proposed by the initiative within three years thereafter by less than twenty-five per centum of the legal voters.

| **2026** | 172,993 | 92,263 | 57,664 | ... initiated constitutional amendment (ICA): 15% of the votes cast for governor ... initiated state statute (ISS): 8% of the votes cast for governor ... veto referendum (VR): 5% of the votes cast for governor
| 2022 | 1,153,284 | ... In Oklahoma, the number of signatures needed to place a measure on the ballot is based on the total number of votes cast for the governor in the preceding election.

H. 1. The total number of signatures collected to meet the requirements for an initiative petition or referendum petition, amending the Oklahoma Statutes, from a single county shall not exceed eleven and five-tenths percent (11.5%) of the number of votes cast in that county during the most recent statewide general election for Governor. ... 2. The total number of signatures collected ... amending the Oklahoma Constitution, from a single county shall not exceed twenty and eight-tenths percent (20.8%) of the number of votes cast in that county during the most recent statewide general election for Governor.

Any person who circulates the petition to collect signatures shall be a registered voter in this state and shall display a conspicuous notice in any location where the person is collecting signatures whether the person is being paid to circulate the petition and if so, by what person or entity. ... I ... say: That I am at least eighteen (18) years old, a registered voter of this state, and that all signatures on the signature sheet were signed in my presence

G. 1. Any person who circulates the petition to collect signatures shall disclose to the Secretary of State any employer or entity that is compensating the person for the circulation of the petition. No compensation shall be based on number of signatures collected, number of signature sheets submitted, or any other similar incentives. No person or entity who does not reside or do business in this state shall contribute to or compensate a person for circulation of a petition.

Pay-per-signature ... Oklahoma prohibits compensation based on the number of signatures collected, the number of petition sheets submitted, "or any other similar incentives." ... Out-of-state circulators ... Oklahoma requires that signature gatherers be registered voters of the state. In 2025, the legislature enacted this requirement.

Oklahoma has a single-subject rule for both initiated statutes and initiated amendments. However, the language of each requirement could be construed as applying only to legislative acts and legislatively referred constitutional amendments. Court cases concering proposed citizen initiatives, however, clarified that the requirement also applied to citizen-initiated measures.

An Act relating to initiative and referendum; amending 34 O.S. 2021, Sections 3, 6, 8 ... which relate to signatures for petitions and ballot title ... adding qualification for persons circulating petition for signatures ... establishing requirements for certain contributions or compensation ... declaring an emergency. ... It being immediately necessary for the preservation of the public peace, health or safety, an emergency is hereby declared to exist, by reason whereof this act shall take effect and be in full force from and after its passage and approval.

In Oklahoma, proponents may circulate a petition for a period of 90 days. For initiatives, the 90-day period for circulation begins on a day set by the secretary of state between 15 and 30 days after all challenges to the initiative petition are finalized. For referendums, signatures are due 90 days after the adjournment of the legislative session in which the targeted law was passed.

voterFile.cost: $500 flat for the statewide voter list (set by statute, ORS 247.945(4) and confirmed on the SOS page). A less-than-statewide (county/partial) list is $25 set-up fee plus 2.5 cents per 100 records.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — "any person." There is no eligibility gate (no candidate/committee/party requirement). Oregon law expressly directs the Secretary of State to deliver a statewide list to "any person" upon request.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Commercial use is prohibited by ORS 247.955: no person supplied a list under ORS 247.940/247.945 "shall use any information in the list for commercial purposes," except resale to candidates or political committees for political purposes only. Campaign/political use (including petition signature verification) is permitted. The file by statute excludes the elector's signature, SSN, driver license number, birth month/day, and Address Confidentiality Program participants (ORS 247.948(2)); candidates/officeholders may suppress their residence address (ORS 247.948(3)).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written/online request to the Oregon Secretary of State, Elections Division (form SEL 510, "Request for Statewide and Less Than Statewide Voter List"). The statewide electronic list is delivered via SFTP; allow 10 business days. Not an instant self-service download. Hardcopy/labels/non-electronic statewide formats are not provided by SOS — those must be requested from county elections officials.

verification.riskNote: Oregon has a robust DIRECT citizen-initiative process for both statutes (6%) and constitutional amendments (8%), plus a citizen veto referendum (4%), all keyed to the votes cast for all candidates for Governor at the most recent 4-year-term gubernatorial election. Current cycle base = 2022 governor total of 2,006,888 votes, producing the 2024/2026 targets: ICA 160,551 (8% = 160,551.04), IS 120,413 (6% = 120,413.28), veto referendum 80,276 (4% = 80,275.52) valid signatures. citations.quote: An initiative law may be proposed only by a petition signed by a number of qualified voters equal to six percent of the total number of votes cast for all candidates for Governor at the election at which a Governor was elected for a term of four years next preceding the filing of the petition.

An initiative amendment to the Constitution may be proposed only by a petition signed by a number of qualified voters equal to eight percent of the total number of votes cast for all candidates for Governor at the election at which a Governor was elected for a term of four years next preceding the filing of the petition.

A referendum on an Act or part thereof may be ordered by a petition signed by a number of qualified voters equal to four percent of the total number of votes cast for all candidates for Governor at the election at which a Governor was elected for a term of four years next preceding the filing of the petition. A referendum petition shall be filed not more than 90 days after the end of the session at which the Act is passed.

An initiative petition shall include the full text of the proposed law or amendment to the Constitution. A proposed law or amendment to the Constitution shall embrace one subject only and matters properly connected therewith.
An initiative petition shall be filed not less than four months before the election at which the proposed law or amendment to the Constitution is to be voted upon.

For a constitutional initiative, valid signatures totaling at least 8% of the total votes cast for governor at the last election is required. For a statutory initiative, valid signatures totaling at least 6% of the total votes cast for governor at the last election is required.
For a referendum petition, valid signatures totaling at least 4% of the total votes cast for governor at the last election is required
Signatures must be submitted to the Elections Division for verification no later than four months prior to the date of the next regularly scheduled general election.
After gathering the required number of signatures, chief petitioners submit them to the Elections Division for verification, no later than 90 days after the Legislature adjourns sine die.

For measures targeting the November 3, 2026, ballot, the deadline is Wednesday, July 2, 2026. If signatures are submitted at least 165 days before an election and the petition is found insufficient, additional signatures can be submitted prior to the final deadline. The signature verification deadline is August 2, 2026.

Text of Section 1b:  Payment for Signatures   It shall be unlawful to pay or receive money or other thing of value based on the number of signatures obtained on an initiative or referendum petition. Nothing herein prohibits payment for signature gathering which is not based, either directly or indirectly, on the number of signatures obtained.
Oregon prohibits paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.
Oregon does not require petition circulators to reside in the state.
The prospective petition for a state measure to be initiated: (A) Shall contain the signatures of at least 1,000 electors; and (B) May not contain the signatures of more than 2,000 electors.

After chief petitioners gather and submit 1,000 sponsorship signatures, the Elections Division forwards the text of the prospective petition to the attorney general for the drafting of a ballot title, which impartially summarizes the petition and its major effect.

Yes. SD Const., Art. XXIII, Sec. 1 (Amendment Z, 2018) requires initiated constitutional amendments to embrace only one subject and that multiple proposed amendments be voted on separately. SoS reviews each proposed measure for single-subject compliance (SB 92, 2025); must also be an amendment, not a constitutional revision.

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Per-signature pay for circulators is explicitly BANNED. SDCL 12-13-28: 'No person may employ, reward, or compensate any person to circulate a petition for an initiated measure, referred law, or proposed amendment to the South Dakota Constitution based on the number of registered voters who signed the petition.' Violation = Class 2 misdemeanor. HOWEVER, the statute expressly per

voterFile.cost: $225 for the electronic statewide data file of all registered voters (SDCL 12-4-39(5)(f) cap). Paper statewide list up to $1,000; legislative district up to $100; county/subdivision up to $50; partial/update lists $75-$100. Make check payable to "SD Secretary of State." Note: an Aug 12, 2025 administrative-rules hearing was scheduled to revise this cost structure, so confirm the current electronic-file fee with the SOS Elections office.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Available to the public but issuance is conditioned on a signed permitted-use certification and disclosure of the political/non-commercial user. The request form requires naming "the candidate, ballot question or party committee utilizing the voter registration file." Statewide lists are pulled only by the Secretary of State's office; county/legislative/municipal/school sublists must be requested from the relevant county auditor.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strict, statutory (SDCL 12-4-41): voter registration information "may not be sold, may not be used for any commercial purpose, and may not be placed for unrestricted access on the internet." Crucially, "'commercial purpose' does not include campaign or political polling activities" — so campaign/petition signature-verification use is permitted. Penalty: Class 1 misdemeanor plus a civil penalty up to $2,000 per violation. The requester must sign a sworn acknowledgment of these limits (no electronic signature font allowed).

signatures.headline: Utah Code 20A-7-201(2)(a): 'legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in the state on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election' (base = active voters as of January 1, 2025, the Jan 1 after the Nov 2024 general election). NOTE: the base is ACTIVE VOTERS, not votes cast for an office.

NOTE: the base is ACTIVE VOTERS, not votes cast for an office.
rules.note: direct/indirect: direct (submitted to a vote of the people)

rules.value: 20A-7-105(5)(a)(i): signed/verified packets must be submitted to the county clerk before 5 p.m. no later than the EARLIER of (A) the first business day at least 30 calendar days after the first signature; (B) the last business day no more than 316 calendar days after the application is filed; or (C) 'the February 15 immediately before the next regular general election immediate

From at least 26 of the 29 Utah State Senate districts, signatures equal to 8% of the active voters in that district on the same Jan 1 (20A-7-201(2)(a)(ii)). Per the Lt. Governor's official 2026 figures, the per-district 8% thresholds range from 2,975 (Senate 10) to 5,715 (Senate 19).

Yes. 20A-7-202(5)(a)(iv) requires the LG to reject an initiative application that 'contains more than one subject,' and 20A-7-202(6) applies 'the same standard provided in Utah Constitution, Article VI, Section 22, which prohibits a bill from passing that contains more than one subject.' Also 20A-7-202(5)(b): subject must be clearly expressed in the law's title.

compensation.operatingStance: per-signature pay BANNED for initiatives. Utah BANS per-signature pay for initiative AND referendum petitions; compensation must be hourly. Utah Code 20A-1-1004(1) circulator verification sheet (eff. 5/6/2026) states verbatim: "I understand that if I have been or will be paid to gather signatures for this petition, my compensation must be based on an hourly rate if the petition is an initiative or referendum petition,

voterFile.cost: $1,050.00 flat for a one-time statewide purchase (no subscriptions, updates, or refunds). Separate election-period reports (Who Has Voted / Who Has Been Sent an Absentee Ballot) are $35.00 per election each; precinct-level results are free.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public. Per the Lt. Governor's Office, "portions of a voter registration form is available to the public, unless a voter has requested that his or her voter information is classified as an At-Risk record." No candidate/party/registered-user gating for the public statewide file.
voterFile.useRestrictions: At-Risk-classified voter records are withheld: "These records cannot be provided to members of the public, political parties, or candidates." Universally protected data (DOB, driver license / state ID / SSN, and—absent voter consent beginning Jan 2027—email and phone) is never released to the public. The statewide file's contents and release are governed by Utah Code 20A-2-602 (contents), 20A-2-601 (age range only, not exact DOB), and 20A-2-607 (timing). County/district lists must be obtained from the relevant county clerk(s).
voterFile.requestProcess: Online request form submitted to the Lieutenant Governor's Office at https://cs.utah.gov/s/elections-request. Not an instant download: "You will receive an email that allows you to download the database within one business day of your purchase." Note the pre-election blackout under Utah Code 20A-2-607 (see note).

The current-cycle valid-signature requirements (140,749 for the 8% direct initiative and referendum; 70,373 for the 4% indirect initiative) are taken verbatim from the Lt. Governor's official '2026 Signature Requirements for Statewide Petitions in Utah' PDF, computed from the statutory base of 1,759,353 active voters as of January 1, 2025 (the Jan 1 following the Nov 2024 general election). The formula, distribution

citations.quote: The legal voters of the State of Utah, in the numbers, under the conditions, in the manner, and within the time provided by statute, may: (A) initiate any desired legislation and cause it to be submitted to the people for adoption upon a majority vote of those voting on the legislation, as provided by statute; or (B) require any law passed by the Legislature, except those laws passed by a two-thirds vote of the members elected to each house of the Legislature, to be submitted to the voters of the State, as provided by statute, before the law may take effect.

Notwithstanding Subsection (2)(a)(i)(A), initiated legislation that is subject to a vote that occurs after November 1, 2026, shall be adopted upon approval of at least 60% of those voting on the legislation if the initiated legislation includes: (A) the imposition of a new tax; ... Takes effect on November 23, 2026, if approved by a majority of those voting on it at the general election on November 3, 2026.

A person seeking to have an initiative submitted to a vote of the people for approval or rejection shall, after filing an initiative application, obtain: (i) legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in the state on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election; and (ii) from at least 26 Utah State Senate districts, legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in that district on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election.

A person seeking to have an initiative submitted to the Legislature for approval or rejection shall, after filing an initiative application, obtain: (i) legal signatures equal to 4% of the number of active voters in the state on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election; and (ii) from at least 26 Utah State Senate districts, legal signatures equal to 4% of the number of active voters in that district on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election.

A regular initiative petition must obtain the signatures of at least 8% of the total number of active voters as of January 1, 2025. Additionally, the initiative must meet the signature thresholds in at least 26 out of 29 Senate districts. ... An Initiative submitted to the legislature must obtain the signatures of at least 4% of the total number of active voters as of January 1, 2025. ... Total 1759353  140749  70373

A person seeking to have a law passed by the Legislature submitted to a vote of the people shall, after filing a referendum application, obtain: (i) legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in the state on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election; and (ii) from at least 15 Senate districts, legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in that Senate district on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election.

A referendum petition must obtain the signatures of at least 8% of the total number of active voters as of January 1, 2025. Additionally, a referendum must meet the signature thresholds in at least 15 out of 29 Senate districts.

The sponsors, or an agent of the sponsors, shall submit a signed and verified packet to the county clerk of the county in which the packet was circulated before 5 p.m. no later than the earlier of: (i) for a statewide initiative: (A) the first business day that is at least 30 calendar days after the day on which the first individual signs the initiative packet; (B) the last business day that is no more than 316 calendar days after the day on which the application for the initiative petition is filed; or (C) the February 15 immediately before the next regular general election immediately after the application is filed under Section 20A-7-202
February 15 was the final day signatures could be submitted. ... County clerks had until March 8, 2026 at the latest to complete verification.

Individuals wishing to circulate a referendum petition shall file a referendum application with the lieutenant governor no later than 5 p.m. on the first business day that is at least five calendar days after the day on which the legislative session at which the law passed ends.

citations.field: referendum packet deadline (40 days after session)
citations.quote: for a statewide referendum: (A) the first business day that is at least 30 calendar days after the day on which the first individual signs the referendum packet; or (B) the first business day that is at least 40 calendar days after the day on which the legislative session at which the law passed ends

The lieutenant governor shall reject an initiative application ... and not issue signature sheets if: (a) the proposed law: ... (iv) contains more than one subject as evaluated in accordance with Subsection (6) ... To evaluate whether the proposed law contains more than one subject under Subsection (5)(a)(iv), the lieutenant governor shall apply the same standard provided in Utah Constitution, Article VI, Section 22, which prohibits a bill from passing that contains more than one subject.

I understand that if I have been or will be paid to gather signatures for this petition, my compensation must be based on an hourly rate if the petition is an initiative or referendum petition, or on an hourly rate or rate per valid signature if the petition is another type of petition.

citations.quote: The legal voters of the State of Utah, in the numbers, under the conditions, in the manner, and within the time provided by statute, may: (A) initiate any desired legislation and cause it to be submitted to the people for adoption upon a majority vote of those voting on the legislation, as provided by statute; or (B) require any law passed by the Legislature, except those laws passed by a two-thirds vote of the members elected to each house of the Legislature, to be submitted to the voters of the State, as provided by statute, before the law may take effect.

Notwithstanding Subsection (2)(a)(i)(A), initiated legislation that is subject to a vote that occurs after November 1, 2026, shall be adopted upon approval of at least 60% of those voting on the legislation if the initiated legislation includes: (A) the imposition of a new tax; ... Takes effect on November 23, 2026, if approved by a majority of those voting on it at the general election on November 3, 2026.

A person seeking to have an initiative submitted to a vote of the people for approval or rejection shall, after filing an initiative application, obtain: (i) legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in the state on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election; and (ii) from at least 26 Utah State Senate districts, legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in that district on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election.

A person seeking to have an initiative submitted to the Legislature for approval or rejection shall, after filing an initiative application, obtain: (i) legal signatures equal to 4% of the number of active voters in the state on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election; and (ii) from at least 26 Utah State Senate districts, legal signatures equal to 4% of the number of active voters in that district on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election.

A regular initiative petition must obtain the signatures of at least 8% of the total number of active voters as of January 1, 2025. Additionally, the initiative must meet the signature thresholds in at least 26 out of 29 Senate districts. ... An Initiative submitted to the legislature must obtain the signatures of at least 4% of the total number of active voters as of January 1, 2025. ... Total 1759353  140749  70373

A person seeking to have a law passed by the Legislature submitted to a vote of the people shall, after filing a referendum application, obtain: (i) legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in the state on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election; and (ii) from at least 15 Senate districts, legal signatures equal to 8% of the number of active voters in that Senate district on January 1 immediately following the last regular general election.

A referendum petition must obtain the signatures of at least 8% of the total number of active voters as of January 1, 2025. Additionally, a referendum must meet the signature thresholds in at least 15 out of 29 Senate districts.

The sponsors, or an agent of the sponsors, shall submit a signed and verified packet to the county clerk of the county in which the packet was circulated before 5 p.m. no later than the earlier of: (i) for a statewide initiative: (A) the first business day that is at least 30 calendar days after the day on which the first individual signs the initiative packet; (B) the last business day that is no more than 316 calendar days after the day on which the application for the initiative petition is filed; or (C) the February 15 immediately before the next regular general election immediately after the application is filed under Section 20A-7-202
February 15 was the final day signatures could be submitted. ... County clerks had until March 8, 2026 at the latest to complete verification.

Individuals wishing to circulate a referendum petition shall file a referendum application with the lieutenant governor no later than 5 p.m. on the first business day that is at least five calendar days after the day on which the legislative session at which the law passed ends.

signatures.headline: Initiative to the People (DIRECT initiated statute). Const. Art. II Sec. 1(a): 'the number of valid signatures of legal voters required shall be equal to eight percent of the votes cast for the office of governor at the last gubernatorial election preceding the initial filing of the text of the initiative measure with the secretary of state.' Base = votes cast for governor at the 2024 general election (~3,861,387).

deadline.headline: Initiatives to the People: January 5, 2026 - July 2, 2026 (per SoS filing deadlines page). Constitutional rule: 'Initiative petitions shall be filed with the secretary of state not less than four months before the election at which they are to be voted upon.'

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature (pay-per-signature) compensation of circulators is ALLOWED for initiatives. Washington statute (former RCW 29.79.490, recodified at RCW 29A.84.250) on its face prohibited paying circulators on a per-signature basis, but that ban was struck down as unconstitutional in LIMIT v. Maleng, 874 F. Supp. 1138 (W.D. Wash. 1994), and is unenforceable. Washington thus is NOT

voterFile.cost: Free in practice. No fee is shown or collected on the request form; the governing statute requires lists be furnished "at actual reproduction cost," which for the emailed electronic CSV extract is effectively $0. Conservatively: free / actual reproduction cost.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — the WA Secretary of State "shall promptly furnish current lists of registered voters in his or her possession, at actual reproduction cost, to any person requesting such information" (RCW 29A.08.720(2)). No candidate/committee/registered-user eligibility gate.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strong. The list "shall not be used for the purpose of mailing or delivering any advertisement or offer... or... any solicitation for money, services, or anything of value." However "the lists and labels may be used for any political purpose" — defined to include support of/opposition to candidates and ballot measures — which covers petition-signature verification for a campaign. Penalty for prohibited (commercial/solicitation) use is a class C felony (up to 5 years imprisonment and/or $10,000 fine) plus $5-per-item civil liability and possible class action (RCW 29A.08.740).
voterFile.requestProcess: Online webform on the SOS "Washington Voter Registration Database Extract" page. Requester submits name, email, phone, and mailing address and checks a box agreeing to lawful use under RCW 29A.08.720, 29A.08.740, and 42.56.070(8). "You will be sent an email with a download link upon approval." Fulfillment "can take up to five business days... in most cases, the request is satisfied immediately." No notarized affidavit; just a click-through use agreement.

citations.quote: (a) Initiative: The first power reserved by the people is the initiative. Every such petition shall include the full text of the measure so proposed. In the case of initiatives to the legislature and initiatives to the people, the number of valid signatures of legal voters required shall be equal to eight percent of the votes cast for the office of governor at the last gubernatorial election preceding the initial filing of the text of the initiative measure with the secretary of state.

Initiative petitions shall be filed with the secretary of state not less than four months before the election at which they are to be voted upon, or not less than ten days before any regular session of the legislature.

The number of valid signatures of registered voters required on a petition for referendum of an act of the legislature or any part thereof, shall be equal to or exceeding four percent of the votes cast for the office of governor at the last gubernatorial election preceding the filing of the text of the referendum measure with the secretary of state.

Referendum petitions against measures passed by the legislature shall be filed with the secretary of state not later than ninety days after the final adjournment of the session of the legislature which passed the measure on which the referendum is demanded.
The number of signatures required for an initiative is 308,911 and the number for a referendum is 154,455.
Initiatives to the People: January 5, 2026 - July 2, 2026; Initiatives to the Legislature: March 11, 2026 - December 31, 2026; Referendum: *March 12, 2026 - *June 10, 2026
Washington does not have a distribution requirement for petition signatures.
Washington has a single-subject rule for ballot measures. The relevant law restricts bills in the legislature. However, this restriction has been found to apply to initiatives as well.
Washington does not require signature gatherers to be residents of the state.
Washington allows paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. Statute provides for such a ban, but the law was struck down in LIMIT v. Maleng in 1994.

Single-subject rule (Art. II Sec. 19) is directed at legislation/initiatives; a referendum refers an existing legislative act, so the single-subject constraint attaches to the underlying act rather than being imposed anew on the referendum petition.

compensation.operatingStance: Per-signature (pay-per-signature) compensation of circulators is ALLOWED for initiatives. Washington statute (former RCW 29.79.490, recodified at RCW 29A.84.250) on its face prohibited paying circulators on a per-signature basis, but that ban was struck down as unconstitutional in LIMIT v. Maleng, 874 F. Supp. 1138 (W.D. Wash. 1994), and is unenforceable. Washington thus is NOT

voterFile.cost: Free in practice. No fee is shown or collected on the request form; the governing statute requires lists be furnished "at actual reproduction cost," which for the emailed electronic CSV extract is effectively $0. Conservatively: free / actual reproduction cost.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — the WA Secretary of State "shall promptly furnish current lists of registered voters in his or her possession, at actual reproduction cost, to any person requesting such information" (RCW 29A.08.720(2)). No candidate/committee/registered-user eligibility gate.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strong. The list "shall not be used for the purpose of mailing or delivering any advertisement or offer... or... any solicitation for money, services, or anything of value." However "the lists and labels may be used for any political purpose" — defined to include support of/opposition to candidates and ballot measures — which covers petition-signature verification for a campaign. Penalty for prohibited (commercial/solicitation) use is a class C felony (up to 5 years imprisonment and/or $10,000 fine) plus $5-per-item civil liability and possible class action (RCW 29A.08.740).
voterFile.requestProcess: Online webform on the SOS "Washington Voter Registration Database Extract" page. Requester submits name, email, phone, and mailing address and checks a box agreeing to lawful use under RCW 29A.08.720, 29A.08.740, and 42.56.070(8). "You will be sent an email with a download link upon approval." Fulfillment "can take up to five business days... in most cases, the request is satisfied immediately." No notarized affidavit; just a click-through use agreement.

citations.quote: (a) Initiative: The first power reserved by the people is the initiative. Every such petition shall include the full text of the measure so proposed. In the case of initiatives to the legislature and initiatives to the people, the number of valid signatures of legal voters required shall be equal to eight percent of the votes cast for the office of governor at the last gubernatorial election preceding the initial filing of the text of the initiative measure with the secretary of state.

Initiative petitions shall be filed with the secretary of state not less than four months before the election at which they are to be voted upon, or not less than ten days before any regular session of the legislature.

The number of valid signatures of registered voters required on a petition for referendum of an act of the legislature or any part thereof, shall be equal to or exceeding four percent of the votes cast for the office of governor at the last gubernatorial election preceding the filing of the text of the referendum measure with the secretary of state.

Referendum petitions against measures passed by the legislature shall be filed with the secretary of state not later than ninety days after the final adjournment of the session of the legislature which passed the measure on which the referendum is demanded.
The number of signatures required for an initiative is 308,911 and the number for a referendum is 154,455.
Initiatives to the People: January 5, 2026 - July 2, 2026; Initiatives to the Legislature: March 11, 2026 - December 31, 2026; Referendum: *March 12, 2026 - *June 10, 2026
Washington does not have a distribution requirement for petition signatures.
Washington has a single-subject rule for ballot measures. The relevant law restricts bills in the legislature. However, this restriction has been found to apply to initiatives as well.
Washington does not require signature gatherers to be residents of the state.
Washington allows paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. Statute provides for such a ban, but the law was struck down in LIMIT v. Maleng in 1994.

voterFile.cost: Statewide File $125; Party $50; County $35; Legislative District $25 (statewide requests, per official fee schedule on the order form). County-level requests: fee set by the local County Clerk.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Political use only, eligibility-gated per W.S. 22-2-113(a): candidates for political office, candidate campaign committees, political party central committees and officials, elected officials, political action committees, individuals promoting/opposing a ballot issue or candidate, and organizations promoting voter participation. NOT available to the general public or for commercial use.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political purposes only; commercial use prohibited. Affiant must affirm they will not use the data for commercial purposes and, if redistributing, must notify subsequent parties of the commercial-use prohibition. Violations of W.S. 22-2-113(a) are referred for prosecution as a misdemeanor under W.S. 22-26-112.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form (Wyoming Voter Registry List Request Form, VoterProductOrderForm.pdf) emailed to elections@wyo.gov or mailed to the Election Division. Requires a signed oath/affidavit under penalty of perjury affirming political-use-only, plus documentary evidence of eligibility (e.g., PAC formation/officers). Payment (cash or check payable to the Wyoming Secretary of State) must be received before data is delivered. For county/municipal officials/candidates/PACs/parties, the request goes to the local County Clerk instead.

citations.quote: Sec. 52. Initiative and referendum. (a) The people may propose and enact laws by the initiative, and approve or reject acts of the legislature by the referendum.

The people may propose and enact laws by the initiative process. It is highly recommended that before beginning this process the applicants consult the Wyoming Constitution and the Election Code of the Wyoming State Statutes.
(i) It is signed by qualified voters, equal in number to fifteen percent (15%) of those who voted in the preceding general election; and
Initiative Signatures Required: 40,669 — 15% of total votes cast in 2024 general election: 271,123 x .15 = 40,669
Distribution requirement: 15% of those voting in 2024 general election in at least two-thirds (2/3rds) of counties = 16 counties

(ii) It is signed by qualified voters equal in number to fifteen percent (15%) of those resident in at least two-thirds (2/3) of the counties of the state, as determined by those who voted in the preceding general election in that county.
The committee has 18 months from the date petitions are provided to file them with the Secretary of State.

The secretary of state shall prepare a ballot title and proposition summarizing the proposed law, and shall place them on the ballot for the first statewide election held more than one hundred twenty (120) days after adjournment of the legislative session following the filing.
The bill may not contain more than one subject. The bill may not dedicate revenues. The bill may not make or repeal appropriations.

(e) A referendum petition may be filed only within ninety (90) days after adjournment of the legislative session at which the act was passed, except that a referendum petition respecting any act previously passed by the legislature may be filed within six months after the power of referendum is adopted.
The committee must submit registered voter signatures representing 15% of those who voted in the preceding general election and 15% of those resident in at least two-thirds of Wyoming counties.

If the petition is determined to be properly filed, the referendum is placed on the ballot of the first general election held more than 180 days after adjournment of the legislative session at which the act was passed.

Circulators may not be paid based upon the number of signatures gathered, nor shall a circulator pay or offer to pay any compensation to another person for that person's signature. If circulators will be paid to solicit signatures, a statement disclosing that fact must appear on the petition.

22-24-323. Petitions; payment for signatures; misrepresentation of petition; prohibition; penalty. (a) A circulator of an initiative petition or a person who causes the circulation of an initiative petition may not pay or offer to pay any compensation to another person for that person's signature on the initiative petition.
Only qualified sponsors/circulators may circulate petitions... Circulators must be U.S. citizens and at least 18 years old. Circulators do not have to be registered voters.
Governance: Wyoming Constitution Article 3 Section 52 and W.S. 22-24-301 through 22-24-323
